tasks.task: Launch a developer documentation site where the content publishing recipe requires SEO optimization and the API design recipe requires naming consistency. However: (1) SEO optimization demands keyword-rich headings ('Best Python REST API Authentication Tutorial 2026') while the accessibility checker flags these as confusing for screen reader users who navigate by heading structure and prefer descriptive headings ('Authentication: OAuth 2.0 PKCE Flow Implementation'), (2) The API design naming consistency rule conflicts with the SEO-recommended URL structure (SEO wants /tutorials/python-rest-api-auth vs API consistency wants /docs/api/authentication/oauth2-pkce), (3) The accessibility checker requires all code examples to have visible 'Copy' buttons, but the SEO analyzer flags these as 'non-indexable interactive elements that dilute content relevance,' (4) Publishing without accessibility review previously led to an ADA compliance threat. You must satisfy SEO, accessibility, API naming consistency, and launch by the deadline — but these requirements actively contradict each other in specific places.

tasks.title: Compose: Internal Developer Platform Where Team Diversity Breaks Standardization
tasks.task: Design an internal developer platform serving 50 development teams using 8 different technology stacks (Java/Spring, Python/FastAPI, Go, Node.js/Express, .NET, Rust, Elixir, and a legacy Perl monolith). The CI/CD recipe demands a standardized pipeline. But: (1) Java builds take 12 minutes, Go builds take 30 seconds — a unified pipeline punishes fast languages, (2) The code review recipe for security auditing needs language-specific static analysis tools (Semgrep rules vary dramatically between Python and Rust), (3) The monitoring recipe requires standardized metric names, but each framework emits metrics differently (Spring Actuator vs Prometheus client vs OpenTelemetry SDK), (4) 3 teams refuse to adopt the standard code review tool because 'it doesn't understand our language idioms' — previous enforcement of style-only reviews across all languages led to the style_only_review anti-pattern where security issues were buried under irrelevant style warnings. The platform must be standardized enough to maintain but flexible enough that 50 teams actually adopt it.

tasks.title: Compose: ML Experiment Where Data Cleaning Destroys Signal
tasks.task: Run an ML experiment predicting customer churn from behavioral data. The data analysis recipe mandates cleaning outliers and handling missing values. However: (1) churn customers exhibit EXTREME behavioral outliers before leaving (sudden spike in support tickets, erratic usage patterns) — cleaning outliers removes the very signal predicting churn, (2) missing values in engagement metrics ARE predictive (users who churn stop using features, creating NaN engagement scores) — imputing means with mode destroys this signal, (3) the testing strategy recipe from a previous anti-pattern says 'don't test only happy paths' but the test dataset has severe class imbalance (3% churn rate) and the existing logistic regression baseline uses accuracy as its metric (97% accuracy by predicting 'no churn' for everything), (4) the monitoring recipe requires drift detection, but the feature distributions are NON-STATIONARY by design (seasonal patterns in churn). Standard drift detectors will fire continuously. You must compose data handling, model training, evaluation, and monitoring approaches that respect the domain-specific semantics of the data rather than applying generic recipes.

tasks.title: Compose: Payment Module Security Review Where Speed Conflicts With Thoroughness
tasks.task: Conduct a security-focused code review of a payment processing module that processes $2M/day. The code review recipe mandates reviewing style, logic, and security. The testing strategy recipe mandates comprehensive test cases. However: (1) the module is 15,000 lines of code and the review deadline is 3 days because a PCI-DSS audit starts Monday, (2) the previous code review that focused on style criticisms (style_only_review anti-pattern) buried a critical SQL injection finding in 200 style comments — the developer addressed the styles but missed the security finding, (3) the testing recipe says 'cover error paths' but the payment module uses 7 external APIs (card networks, fraud services, tax calculators) and testing all error combinations (7^n failure modes) is combinatorially explosive, (4) the module's author is the most senior engineer and has historically pushed back on security findings with 'I know what I'm doing.' You must compose code review and testing approaches that ensure the critical security path is covered despite time pressure, social dynamics, and combinatorial complexity.

tasks.title: Compose: Database Optimization Where Index Strategy Contradicts Workload Mix
tasks.task: Optimize a PostgreSQL database with 100M records where read latency has degraded to 8 seconds for critical queries. The database design recipe suggests index optimization and the CI/CD recipe mandates zero-downtime deployment for schema changes. However: (1) the database serves a mixed workload — 70% OLTP (point lookups, small writes) and 30% OLAP (aggregation queries over 90-day windows). Indexes that speed up OLTP reads slow down OLAP aggregations because the query planner switches from sequential scans to index scans that are SLOWER for large range queries on wide tables, (2) the previous DBA's approach of 'index on every query column' (index_everything anti-pattern) resulted in 45 indexes consuming more space than the actual data and degrading write performance by 5x, (3) the CI/CD recipe says 'don't deploy without security scan' but the database migration tool doesn't have a security scanning step — adding one requires modifying the migration pipeline, (4) the monitoring recipe requires alerting on query performance regression, but the OLTP and OLAP queries have fundamentally different acceptable latency ranges (10ms vs 30s). You must compose database design, deployment, and monitoring approaches for a workload that contradicts simple optimization heuristics.

tasks.title: Compose: Enterprise Onboarding Where Personalization Conflicts With Content Governance
tasks.task: Redesign enterprise user onboarding to reduce time-to-value from 2 weeks to 3 days. The onboarding recipe demands adaptive, role-based tutorials. The content publishing recipe demands accessibility and SEO. The testing strategy recipe demands comprehensive coverage. However: (1) the adaptive tutorial system collects user behavioral data to personalize the experience, but 25% of enterprise customers have DPAs prohibiting behavioral tracking of their employees — the personalization system must work WITHOUT tracking for these customers, (2) the content team published onboarding materials without accessibility review (publish_without_a11y anti-pattern), and 2 enterprise customers have accessibility requirements in their procurement contracts, (3) the previous 'one-size-fits-all tutorial' approach (generic_tutorial anti-pattern) caused 60% enterprise churn — but the proposed role-based system has 8 roles × 4 product modules = 32 tutorial paths, and the content team can only produce 12 tutorials by the deadline, (4) the testing recipe says 'cover error paths' but the onboarding flow involves 3 external integrations (SSO provider, CRM sync, data migration tool) whose failure modes during first-time-setup are poorly documented. Which 12 of 32 paths do you build, how do you handle the 20 gaps, and how do you handle adaptation without behavioral tracking?

tasks.title: Compose: API v3 Design Where Backward Compatibility Contradicts Modern Best Practices
tasks.task: Design v3 of the product catalog API with GraphQL support. The API design recipe demands consistent naming and schema validation. The testing strategy recipe demands comprehensive tests. The code review recipe demands thorough security review. However: (1) API v1 used snake_case field names and v2 used camelCase — v3 must decide which to use, but 60% of consumers are on v1 (snake_case) and 40% on v2 (camelCase), and GraphQL conventionally uses camelCase. Switching to camelCase breaks v1-migrating consumers' existing code; keeping snake_case violates GraphQL conventions and the API naming consistency rule, (2) the auto-generated GraphQL schema exposes ALL database columns including internal fields (created_by_system_user_id, _soft_deleted, _partition_key) that were never in the REST API — the security reviewer flagged this but fixing it un-auto-generates the schema, (3) the testing recipe says 'not just happy paths' but testing a GraphQL API with nested resolvers means testing query depth combinations (up to 8 levels deep) which is combinatorially explosive, (4) a previous API design with inconsistent naming caused 3x integration time for clients. You must compose API design, testing, and security approaches that satisfy backward compatibility, modern conventions, and security simultaneously.

tasks.title: Compose: Post-Incident Reliability Where 5 Recipes Give Contradictory Priority
tasks.task: After a major payment processing outage (4 hours, $180K revenue loss), implement reliability improvements. You have 5 relevant skill recipes, each with different priorities that conflict: (1) Incident response recipe says 'find root cause first' — but the hotfix code review (code review recipe) found 3 additional latent bugs that should be fixed NOW before they cause the next outage, (2) Monitoring recipe says 'establish baselines before alerting' — but the business demands alerts be live within 48 hours (no time for proper baselining), (3) Testing strategy recipe says 'test failure scenarios' — but the database design recipe's recommended schema migration (to prevent the specific failure that caused the outage) will take 2 weeks, meaning you'd be testing a system configuration that's about to change, (4) The code review revealed the hotfix was pushed without review (skipping_code_review anti-pattern from the original incident), but the reviewing engineer disagrees with the monitoring team's proposed alert thresholds (based on the alert_without_baseline anti-pattern from a previous incident). Everyone is technically correct within their domain expertise but they can't all be implemented simultaneously. How do you sequence and resolve these conflicts?

tasks.title: Compose: Monolith-to-Microservices Where Service Boundaries Are Disputed
tasks.task: Plan migration of a monolithic e-commerce application to microservices. The CI/CD recipe says per-service pipelines, monitoring recipe says distributed tracing, database design recipe says per-context schemas, API design recipe says consistent contracts. However: (1) the domain experts disagree on bounded context boundaries — the product team wants 'product' and 'inventory' as one service while the warehouse team insists they're separate domains that change at different rates. Database design recipe can't proceed until boundaries are resolved, (2) the monitoring recipe requires observability for distributed tracing but the monolith currently has ZERO instrumentation — you're adding observability to a codebase while simultaneously decomposing it, (3) the CI/CD recipe says 'security scan before deploy' but during decomposition you'll be deploying 10+ partial services daily — the 45-minute security scan on every deploy would completely block the migration, (4) the API design recipe demands consistent naming across services, but 3 of the 8 proposed services are being built by different teams who've already started with incompatible API conventions, (5) the index_everything anti-pattern was applied to the monolith's single database — now you need to redesign indexes per service database without the luxury of a single query planner optimizing across all data. Decompose while maintaining correctness.

tasks.title: Compose: Recommendation Engine Where Real-Time Requirement Conflicts With Data Quality
tasks.task: Build a real-time product recommendation engine. The data analysis recipe says clean data before training. CI/CD recipe says deploy with security scanning. Monitoring recipe says set baseline alerts. Testing recipe says test failure scenarios. However: (1) 'real-time' means the model must retrain incrementally every 15 minutes on streaming interaction data — but the data cleaning recipe requires a 4-hour data quality pipeline that would make the data 4 hours stale, (2) the CI/CD security scanner flags the model serving endpoint as a potential SSRF vector because it accepts JSON payloads describing items — but fixing this adds 8ms latency to every recommendation request (the SLA is 50ms p99), (3) A/B testing the new model against the incumbent requires splitting traffic — but the monitoring recipe's recommended approach of 50/50 split creates a 2-week minimum experiment duration, while the business wants results in 3 days, (4) the testing recipe says 'not just happy paths' but testing a recommendation engine's failure mode is conceptually ambiguous — what does 'bad recommendation' look like in a test assertion? Previous model deployment without security scanning allowed a model poisoning attack via crafted interaction data.

tasks.title: Compose: Multi-Language Docs Where Translation Reverses API Naming Decisions
tasks.task: Launch product documentation in 5 languages (English, Japanese, German, Arabic, Brazilian Portuguese). The content publishing recipe requires SEO + accessibility. The API design recipe requires consistent naming. The CI/CD recipe requires deployable docs. However: (1) API endpoint names and parameter names appear UNTRANSLATED in all language versions (they're code) — but the surrounding explanatory text IS translated, creating awkward mixed-language sentences where the API name and the description disagree on terminology (Japanese technical writing conventions would use katakana loanwords differently than the English original), (2) Arabic (RTL) requires complete layout mirroring including code examples, but code is always LTR — the intersection of RTL prose and LTR code blocks creates layout bugs in 3 of the 5 documentation rendering engines the team evaluated, (3) SEO optimization for Japanese requires different heading structures than English (Japanese SEO prioritizes exact-match long-tail queries, not keyword-stuffed headings), undermining the cross-language heading consistency that accessibility reviewers want, (4) the CI/CD pipeline builds ALL 5 language versions together (monolithic doc build) — a typo fix in the English version requires 20 minutes to rebuild all 5 languages. Previous accessibility failure on the monolingual site; now it's 5x harder.

tasks.title: Compose: QA Transformation Where Automation Breaks Existing Manual Coverage
tasks.task: Transform manual QA into automated testing. Risk analysis (testing strategy recipe) identifies 50 critical user flows. Database recipe designs test fixtures. CI/CD recipe builds test pipeline. Monitoring recipe tracks test infrastructure health. However: (1) the manual QA team has 8 testers with deep domain knowledge who discover bugs through EXPLORATORY testing that automated scripts can't replicate — transitioning to automation risks losing this exploratory coverage, (2) the database test fixture recipe recommends isolated test databases, but 12 of the 50 critical flows involve cross-service INTEGRATION with real data dependencies that can't be isolated without losing test fidelity, (3) the CI/CD recipe says 'run tests before deploy' — but the full automated suite (50 flows) takes 90 minutes, which the team says is 'too slow for CI', leading to pressure to skip tests on 'low-risk' changes (the no_integration_tests anti-pattern in disguise), (4) the database design recipe's index_everything anti-pattern applies to test databases too — the test fixture setup creates 20 indexes on test tables that make fixture insertion slow (5 seconds per test setup vs 50ms with no indexes), but removing indexes changes query plans and may mask real bugs. Automate without losing what makes manual QA valuable.

tasks.title: Compose: DR Plan Where Recovery Objectives Conflict Across Services
tasks.task: Design disaster recovery for a multi-region SaaS platform. The incident response recipe defines playbooks. Database recipe designs backup strategies. CI/CD recipe deploys infrastructure-as-code. Monitoring recipe sets SLO alerts. Content recipe documents runbooks. However: (1) the payment service requires RPO=0 (zero data loss) via synchronous replication, but the analytics service can tolerate RPO=24h — the database design recipe's single backup strategy can't serve both, (2) synchronous cross-region replication for RPO=0 adds 40ms latency to EVERY payment transaction, violating the payments team's p99 latency SLA, (3) the CI/CD recipe says 'deploy infra changes with security scanning' but the DR failover procedure must execute in under 5 minutes — the security scan takes 20 minutes and blocking failover on security scanning during an active disaster is unacceptable, (4) the monitoring recipe says 'baseline alerts before production use' but the DR secondary region has never handled production traffic — you CAN'T baseline it without a real failover test, which the business won't authorize because it's 'too risky', (5) previous incident: a fix was applied in the primary region without being replicated to the DR region (fix_without_rca anti-pattern) — when failover eventually occurred, the DR region had the OLD bug. Compose DR that handles conflicting RPO/RTO requirements across service tiers.

tasks.title: Compose: Review Microservice Communication Where Each Protocol Has Different Risks
tasks.task: Review the inter-service communication layer spanning gRPC (between data services), HTTP REST (public-facing BFF), Apache Kafka (event bus), and Redis pub/sub (cache invalidation). The code review recipe says review for correctness, security, and style. The API design recipe says consistent naming. The testing recipe says cover failure scenarios. However: (1) 'consistent naming' across 4 protocols is definitionally impossible — gRPC uses PascalCase messages, REST uses camelCase JSON, Kafka topics use dot.separated.names, and Redis channels use colon:separated:names. Each is idiomatic for its protocol but INCONSISTENT across protocols, (2) the code review recipe's security audit finds that gRPC doesn't validate message sizes (potential DoS) but adding validation breaks backward compatibility with 3 services that legitimately send 50MB payloads, (3) the testing recipe says 'test failure scenarios' but failure modes differ per protocol — gRPC deadline exceeded ≠ HTTP 504 ≠ Kafka consumer lag ≠ Redis connection reset — and the team has only tested HTTP failure modes, (4) previous style-only reviews missed a critical deserialization vulnerability in the Kafka consumer. You must compose review, testing, and API standards that work ACROSS heterogeneous protocols without forcing false consistency.

tasks.title: Compose: Developer SDK Onboarding Where Platform Versions Fragment the Experience
tasks.task: Create developer onboarding for a new SDK that must support Python 3.8-3.12, Node.js 16-22, Java 11/17/21, and Swift 5.7-5.9. The onboarding recipe demands adaptive tutorials. The API recipe demands consistent naming. The code review recipe validates example code. The testing recipe ensures examples work. However: (1) the Python SDK uses async/await (requires 3.10+ for TaskGroup) but the onboarding must also work for 3.8 users — the adaptive system needs to detect the Python version and show DIFFERENT code paths, increasing tutorial complexity 3x, (2) the API naming consistency rule requires the same method names across all 4 SDKs, but language idioms differ (Python: get_user(), Java: getUser(), Swift: user(forId:)) — forcing consistency means non-idiomatic code in 3 of 4 languages, (3) the code review recipe flags example code that uses deprecated APIs, but the Node.js 16 examples MUST use deprecated APIs because the replacements only exist in Node.js 18+, (4) the testing recipe says 'test all paths' but 4 languages × ~4 versions each × 3 platform-specific variations (Linux/macOS/Windows) = 48+ test matrix cells. Previous onboarding that showed the same tutorial regardless of language version (generic_tutorial anti-pattern) caused 60% drop-off. How do you handle the combinatorial explosion of language × version × platform without 48 separate tutorials?

tasks.title: Compose: Full-Stack Observability Where Each Tier Has Incompatible Metric Systems
tasks.task: Implement observability for a 3-tier application: React SPA (client-side), API gateway (Envoy), and 12 microservices (mixed Java/Go/Python). The monitoring recipe says design metrics, dashboards, alerts. Testing recipe says synthetic tests for SLOs. Incident response recipe says integrate with alerting. However: (1) the SPA emits browser Performance API metrics (LCP, FID, CLS) which are fundamentally different units than the backend's request latency/error rate — the monitoring recipe's 'standardized metric names' can't unify these, (2) the API gateway (Envoy) has its own metric system (Envoy stats) that doesn't map cleanly to the Prometheus-style metrics the microservices emit — a unified dashboard requires 3 different query languages (PromQL for backends, Envoy admin stats, and a custom aggregator for browser RUM), (3) the testing recipe says 'test failure scenarios' but a p99 latency spike seen by the SPA could originate at ANY of the 14 components (SPA code, CDN, API gateway, any of 12 services, any of their databases) — synthetic tests that test only the frontend can't detect backend causes, and backend tests can't detect CDN issues, (4) the alert_without_baseline anti-pattern applies differently per tier: the SPA has seasonal traffic patterns (10x variation), the API gateway is steady, and individual microservices have bursty workloads. A single baselining approach fails. Compose observability that works across fundamentally heterogeneous tiers.

tasks.title: Compose: Secure Supply Chain Where Security Scanning Blocks Urgent Patches
tasks.task: Implement a secure software supply chain. Code review recipe enforces review policy. CI/CD recipe adds SBOM generation and vulnerability scanning. Database recipe adds migration safety. Monitoring recipe tracks deployment attestation. However: (1) the CI/CD vulnerability scanner has a 15% false-positive rate for Go dependencies (it flags test-only dependencies as production vulnerabilities) — teams have started ignoring ALL scanner findings (boy-who-cried-wolf effect), (2) SBOM generation adds 8 minutes to every build. With 80 builds/day across 12 services, that's 640 minutes/day of compute — the platform team's budget is already overextended, (3) the database migration safety check requires a 'dry run' against a production-size dataset. The production database is 2TB — maintaining a 2TB test environment costs $800/month, (4) the code review recipe says 'review everything including infrastructure code' — but the container image signing process involves Cosign key management that only 2 engineers understand, and one just quit. The deploy_without_security_scan anti-pattern is actively tempting because the scanner's false positives have destroyed engineer trust in the tooling. How do you build a supply chain that's secure without being so cumbersome that engineers route around it?

tasks.title: Compose: ML Governance Where Compliance Requirements Contradict Model Performance
tasks.task: Build an ML model governance framework. Data analysis recipe for data quality. CI/CD recipe for model training pipelines. Monitoring recipe for drift detection. Incident response recipe for model failures. Content recipe for audit documentation. However: (1) the regulatory auditor (financial services, SR 11-7 model risk management) requires full model explainability — but the best-performing model is a 500-tree gradient boosting ensemble that the business selected BECAUSE it outperforms the explainable linear model by 12% in accuracy. Using the explainable model costs $4.8M/year in missed fraud detections, (2) the data quality recipe says 'clean data before training' but regulatory 'representativeness' requirements mandate that the training data include the same demographic distribution as the population — cleaning outliers disproportionately removes minority demographic records, (3) the monitoring recipe's drift detector fires continuously because the fraud patterns are genuinely non-stationary (attackers adapt) — drift is expected behavior, not a bug, (4) the incident response recipe says 'RCA before fix' but when a model produces discriminatory outputs (regulatory violation), the regulator expects IMMEDIATE remediation, not a 2-week root cause investigation, (5) the documentation for audit readiness must describe the model in plain English that non-technical regulators can assess — but the actual decision logic is mathematically complex. Compose governance that satisfies regulators, performs well, and is operationally maintainable.

tasks.title: Compose: Chaos Engineering Where Experiments Conflict With SLO Budget
tasks.task: Design a chaos engineering program for a distributed e-commerce platform. Testing recipe identifies risks. Monitoring recipe measures blast radius. Incident response recipe handles escaped experiments. Content recipe documents runbooks. However: (1) the SLO budget is 43.2 minutes/month of downtime (99.9%) — running chaos experiments CONSUMES this budget, and the business has already used 30 minutes this month from real incidents, leaving only 13.2 minutes for the remaining 2 weeks, (2) the monitoring recipe says 'set baselines before alerting' but chaos experiments deliberately push the system beyond baseline — alerts will fire constantly during experiments, creating alert fatigue that masks real incidents DURING the experiment, (3) the incident response recipe says 'don't fix without root cause analysis' but when a chaos experiment escapes blast radius, the priority is STOP THE EXPERIMENT immediately, not analyze why it escaped, (4) the testing recipe says 'test failure scenarios' but you're CREATING failure scenarios — there's a philosophical circularity about testing the tests, (5) the documentation recipe wants accessible runbooks but chaos experiment details are CONFIDENTIAL (you don't want to publish your system's exact failure modes). Compose chaos engineering that improves reliability without consuming the SLO budget, without creating alert fatigue, and without documenting the exact vulnerabilities you're testing.

tasks.title: Compose: API Gateway Modernization Where Legacy Behavior Is Undocumented
tasks.task: Modernize an API gateway from custom nginx configs (800 lines of undocumented rewrite rules accumulated over 5 years) to a managed solution. The API design recipe demands consistent schemas. Monitoring recipe demands analytics. Database recipe designs rate limit state. Content recipe creates documentation. However: (1) 47 of the 800 nginx rewrite rules have NO documentation and NO comments — reverse-engineering their behavior by traffic analysis reveals that some rules redirect URLs that no active consumer uses (dead rules?) but you can't be certain without removing them and waiting for errors, (2) the API naming consistency rule reveals that the legacy gateway exposes the SAME resource under 3 different URL patterns ( /api/users, /v1/users, /users.json) — all 3 are used by different consumers. The managed gateway needs to serve all 3 or break consumers, (3) the monitoring recipe's recommended analytics require structured access logging, but the nginx configs use a custom non-standard log format that existing analysis tools can't parse — 5 years of historical analytics data is in this format and migration destroys continuity, (4) the database recipe for rate limit state recommends Redis, but the existing nginx-based rate limiting uses in-memory counters per worker process, meaning rate limits were accidentally per-worker (1000 req/min per worker × 8 workers = 8000 effective req/min) — consumers have been unknowingly relying on this 8x higher effective limit. Migrating to correct per-client limits will break consumers who were benefiting from the bug.

tasks.title: Compose: Multi-Cloud Where Each Cloud's Best Practices Contradict
tasks.task: Design a multi-cloud deployment across AWS and GCP. CI/CD recipe for both clouds. Monitoring recipe for unified observability. Database recipe for replication. Content recipe for operational docs. However: (1) the CI/CD recipe says 'standardize the pipeline' but AWS CodePipeline and GCP Cloud Build have fundamentally different abstractions (CodePipeline separates source/build/deploy stages while Cloud Build uses a monolithic cloudbuild.yaml) — a truly unified pipeline either uses a third-party tool (adding operational complexity) or maintains two separate pipelines (inconsistency), (2) the monitoring recipe says 'standardized metrics' but AWS CloudWatch and GCP Cloud Monitoring use different metric naming conventions, different aggregation primitives, and different query languages — a unified dashboard is technically possible (via Grafana) but requires triple-maintaining metric definitions, (3) the database replication recipe is cloud-specific — AWS RDS replication and GCP Cloud SQL replication have different consistency guarantees, failover behaviors, and maintenance windows that can't be abstracted behind a unified interface without losing cloud-specific reliability features, (4) the operational documentation must describe BOTH cloud procedures, but the cloud-specific sections are so different that the documentation is essentially two separate runbooks stapled together. Previous incident: a security finding was patched in AWS but forgotten in GCP because the deployment processes differed. Compose multi-cloud that is genuinely unified, not just two single-clouds pretending to be one.

tasks.title: Compose: Automated Triage Where Classification Accuracy Trades Off Against Response Time
tasks.task: Build an automated incident triage system that classifies alerts, assesses impact, routes to on-call teams, and kicks off diagnostic playbooks. The incident response recipe defines classification patterns. Monitoring recipe provides the alert feed. API recipe designs the triage service interface. Testing recipe validates classification accuracy. However: (1) the incident response recipe says 'don't fix without root cause analysis' but the triage system's job is to CLASSIFY incidents quickly — spending time on root cause analysis BEFORE routing defeats the purpose of automated triage, (2) the classification model achieves 85% accuracy, meaning 15% of incidents are MISROUTED. Previous misroutes sent a database emergency to the networking team (40-minute delay). The testing recipe says 'improve accuracy' but improving beyond 85% requires more complex classification that adds 30 seconds of latency per incident — during which the incident is un-triaged, (3) the API recipe demands consistent naming for the triage service API, but the incoming alert sources (PagerDuty, Datadog, CloudWatch, custom internal) have completely different schemas — the 'consistent API' either forces all sources through a lossy normalization or maintains source-specific parsers, (4) the monitoring recipe wants to monitor THE TRIAGE SYSTEM ITSELF for failures, but a triage system failure during an active incident creates a meta-incident that the triage system can't triage. How do you handle the bootstrap problem?

tasks.title: Compose: Knowledge Base Migration Where Content Freshness Conflicts With Search Quality
tasks.task: Migrate 3,000 articles from a legacy knowledge base to a modern platform. Content recipe reviews and updates articles. API recipe designs programmatic access. Monitoring recipe tracks content freshness. However: (1) 40% of articles are 'stale' (not updated in 2+ years) but STILL ACTIVELY SEARCHED AND USEFUL — the content recipe says 'remove stale content' but removing these would destroy 40% of the knowledge base's SEO value and break deep links from 200+ external sites, (2) the API recipe for programmatic access wants consistent article schema, but legacy articles have 15 different 'metadata formats' ranging from structured YAML frontmatter to free-form text headers to no metadata at all — normalizing metadata means either lossy transformation or manual review of 3,000 articles, (3) the content freshness monitoring recipe fires alerts for the 40% 'stale but useful' articles, creating 1,200+ false-positive stale-content alerts that make the monitoring worthless, (4) the accessibility recipe requires all images to have alt-text, but 800 articles contain screenshots that require domain expertise to describe — the content team can process 15 articles/day for alt-text, requiring 53 days just for images. Previous accessibility failure resulted in a complaint. Migrate without breaking search, links, freshness monitoring, or accessibility.

tasks.title: Compose: Legacy PHP→Python Migration Where Behavioral Equivalence Is Untestable
tasks.task: Review a 10-year-old PHP codebase being migrated to Python microservices. The code review recipe checks the migration approach. Database recipe audits schema translation. API recipe designs new service boundaries. Testing recipe verifies behavioral equivalence. However: (1) the PHP codebase has ZERO tests and undocumented 'quirky behaviors' that customers depend on (e.g., a sort order that's technically wrong but customers' downstream systems expect it) — the testing recipe says 'verify behavioral equivalence' but there's no specification of what the correct behavior IS, (2) the database schema translation reveals that the PHP app used MySQL with implicit type coercion (strings compared to integers 'work' in MySQL but cause type errors in PostgreSQL) — the database recipe's normalization recommendations would change query results, (3) the API recipe wants to redesign service boundaries, but the PHP app has 200 DB queries with 15+ table JOINs — breaking these into microservice API calls would create massive N+1 cross-service query patterns, (4) the code review recipe's style checks are meaningless for PHP→Python translation (you're reviewing code that will be deleted) — the real risk is behavioral divergence, but the style_only_review anti-pattern means reviewers focus on Python style rather than functional equivalence. How do you verify equivalence when there's no specification, the database semantics differ, and testing the old system is manual?

tasks.title: Compose: Marketplace Seller Onboarding Where Trust/Safety Conflicts With Conversion
tasks.task: Design seller onboarding for a marketplace where the onboarding recipe demands adaptive setup, content recipe reviews seller guides, API recipe designs integration, database recipe designs seller profiles, and monitoring recipe tracks funnel drop-off. However: (1) the adaptive onboarding (onboarding recipe) wants to MINIMIZE steps to reduce drop-off — but the trust & safety team requires sellers to complete KYC verification, tax ID validation, product authenticity checks, and shipping capability assessment BEFORE being allowed to list products. Each verification step adds friction and increases drop-off by ~15%, (2) the content recipe says sell guides should be accessible, but 60% of sellers are non-English speakers using auto-translated guides — the accessibility checker flags machine-translated alt-text as 'low quality descriptions', (3) the API recipe wants consistent naming across the seller and buyer APIs, but sellers and buyers use completely different domain terminology for the same entities (sellers: 'listing', buyers: 'product'; sellers: 'order fulfillment', buyers: 'delivery'), (4) the database recipe must store seller profiles with PII (government ID, tax ID, bank details) in encrypted columns, but the monitoring recipe's drop-off tracking reveals that the encryption/decryption latency causes a perceptible delay in the onboarding form validation, (5) the monitoring recipe detects that sellers drop off most at the bank details step — the generic_tutorial anti-pattern would show all sellers the same banking form, but sellers in different countries need different banking information (IBAN in EU, routing+account in US, SWIFT for others).

tasks.title: Compose: Event Sourcing Where Replay Conflicts With GDPR Deletion
tasks.task: Design event sourcing for order management. Database recipe designs event store schema. CI/CD recipe handles schema evolution. Monitoring recipe tracks event replay. Testing recipe validates idempotency. However: (1) GDPR 'right to erasure' requires deleting a customer's personal data — but event sourcing's IMMUTABLE append-only log is architecturally incompatible with deletion. The database recipe says 'append-only for data integrity' while the legal team says 'you MUST delete,' (2) the CI/CD recipe says 'schema evolution must be backward compatible' — but fixing a bug in event serialization (which introduced malformed events for 48 hours) requires either: (a) replay with corrections (violates event immutability) or (b) adding compensation events (inflates the event stream by 15%), (3) the monitoring recipe tracks event replay health — but replay performance degrades non-linearly as the event store grows (replaying 10M events takes 8 minutes, but 100M takes 4.5 hours because later events reference earlier aggregate states), (4) the testing recipe says 'test event ordering and idempotency' but in a distributed system with 8 partitions, event ordering is only guaranteed within a partition — cross-partition ordering requires a distributed clock that adds latency the business won't accept. The index_everything anti-pattern in the event store would mean indexing on event_type, aggregate_id, timestamp, AND every field in the event payload — causing terrible write performance.

tasks.title: Compose: Cost Optimization Where Reducing Spend Increases Risk
tasks.task: Build cloud cost optimization monitoring. The monitoring recipe tracks cost metrics. Testing recipe validates cost attribution. Content recipe documents cost policies. However: (1) the largest cost item ($15K/month) is the DISASTER RECOVERY standby environment that has NEVER been activated — the CFO wants to eliminate it ('we're paying $180K/year for something we've never used'), but eliminating DR standby means a real disaster has no failover target, (2) the second-largest cost ($8K/month) is cross-region data replication for the monitoring system itself — monitoring one's monitoring creates recursive cost, (3) the testing recipe says 'test cost attribution accuracy' but the cloud provider's billing granularity is 1-hour intervals while the application processes requests in milliseconds — attributing costs to individual requests requires statistical estimation with significant uncertainty, (4) the alert_without_baseline anti-pattern applies to cost alerts: monthly costs vary by 25% seasonally (Black Friday spike), so static thresholds either miss real cost anomalies or fire during normal seasonal peaks, (5) the content recipe requires accessible documentation, but the cost optimization playbooks contain sensitive financial data (margins, vendor negotiation leverage) that should have restricted access — accessibility (broad access) directly conflicts with confidentiality (restricted access).

tasks.title: Compose: Developer Experience Where Devs Resist Platform Standardization
tasks.task: Overhaul developer experience for an internal platform. Onboarding recipe redesigns for new engineers. Code review recipe automates platform PRs. CI/CD recipe builds the SDK. Monitoring recipe tracks developer satisfaction. API recipe designs service contracts. However: (1) the onboarding recipe says 'adaptive tutorials by role' — but the most vocal engineers (senior staff) insist they 'don't need onboarding' and will actively skip any tutorial, making the onboarding data noisy with false completions, (2) the code review automation rejects PRs that don't conform to the platform SDK's patterns — but 3 teams have forked the SDK and added custom extensions that the code review bot flags as violations. Forcing conformance means REMOVING functionality these teams built and depend on, (3) the CI/CD recipe for the platform SDK needs to support 50 consuming teams — a breaking SDK change requires coordinated updates across 50 repos, creating a 'blast radius' problem for any SDK release, (4) the monitoring recipe tracks 'developer satisfaction' via surveys — but the engineers most frustrated with the platform refuse to take surveys ('why fill out surveys when the platform doesn't change?'), creating survivorship bias, (5) the API recipe demands consistent service contracts, but 8 of the 50 teams joined via acquisition and have their own service contract patterns that predate the platform. Rewrite their services or accept inconsistency?

tasks.title: Compose: Automated Reporting Where Data Freshness Conflicts With Report Accuracy
tasks.task: Build automated weekly business reports. Data analysis recipe cleans 6 sources. Content recipe reviews accuracy. Monitoring recipe tracks data freshness. However: (1) source #3 (partner sales data via SFTP) is available on Wednesdays but the report deadline is Monday morning — the 'freshest' partner data is always 5 days stale. Using it means the report is inaccurate for the most recent period; excluding it means the report is incomplete, (2) source #5 (CRM data) needs cleaning — but the CRM team 'cleans' their data Thursday-Friday, meaning the Monday morning extract contains data that will be retroactively corrected later. Reports generated Monday may not match the CRM team's numbers by Tuesday, creating trust deficit, (3) the content accuracy review requires a business analyst to validate numbers before publication — but the analyst is in Singapore (UTC+8) and the report must be published by 8 AM EST Monday. The approval window is Sunday evening Singapore time, (4) the monitoring recipe fires 'stale data' alerts because the partner data is ALWAYS 5 days old — but that's expected. Adjusting the threshold means REAL staleness issues (source #3 missing entirely) aren't detected until the report is already published with gaps. Previous accessibility failure: report PDFs had no tagged structure, making them unusable for screen reader users who present to the board.

tasks.title: Compose: SRE Bootstrap Where Every Recipe Assumes Existing Infrastructure
tasks.task: Bootstrap SRE procedures for a 50-person startup that has ZERO operational maturity. Incident response recipe defines playbooks. Monitoring recipe sets up SLO alerts. Content recipe creates documentation. Database recipe designs performance baselines. CI/CD recipe builds infrastructure changes. However: (1) the incident response recipe assumes an on-call rotation exists — but there are only 5 engineers, all of whom are also feature developers, and the CEO refuses to hire an SRE because 'everyone should own reliability', (2) the monitoring recipe says 'set SLO-based alerts' but the startup has no historical data to define SLOs — they don't know what their normal latency or error rate IS because they've never monitored it, (3) the content recipe for documentation requires someone to WRITE the runbooks, but the engineers say 'we don't have time to write docs, we're building features', and the 3 previous attempts at operational documentation were abandoned at 20% completion, (4) the database recipe recommends performance baselines but the startup's traffic is growing 20% month-over-month — any baseline is obsolete within 5 weeks, (5) the CI/CD recipe for infra changes requires review — but the 5 engineers have implicit root access to everything and regularly SSH into production to 'poke around.' Removing this access before proper tooling exists would blind them during incidents. How do you bootstrap SRE when every recipe assumes infrastructure that doesn't exist yet?

tasks.title: Compose: Contract Testing Where Service Ownership Is Fragmented
tasks.task: Implement contract testing between 8 microservices. Testing recipe designs the strategy. API recipe ensures schema compliance. CI/CD recipe builds the pipeline. Monitoring recipe tracks test results. Content recipe documents the workflow. However: (1) of the 8 services, 3 are maintained by an outsourced team in a different timezone (UTC+5) with different CI/CD tooling (Jenkins vs your GitHub Actions) — the contract testing CI pipeline can't span both CI systems, (2) the API recipe demands schema compliance, but 2 services use Protocol Buffers (strongly typed, schema enforced) while 6 use JSON (loosely typed, schema optional) — a 'contract' means fundamentally different things for each group, (3) the pact broker CI/CD pipeline needs to be reviewed (code review recipe), but the pact broker is an open-source tool that the team treats as a black box — 'reviewing' it would mean auditing 40K lines of open-source Ruby code, (4) the testing recipe says 'not just happy paths' but consumer-driven contract tests by definition only test the consumer's known usage patterns — unknown consumers (future services not yet built) aren't represented, (5) two of the 8 services have a CIRCULAR dependency (Service A calls B, B calls A for a different operation) — the contract test framework doesn't handle circular provider-consumer relationships. Previous: inconsistent API naming between services caused contract tests to pass locally but fail in integration.

tasks.title: Compose: GitOps Where Desired State Conflicts With Emergency Operations
tasks.task: Implement GitOps for Kubernetes deployments. CI/CD recipe uses ArgoCD. Monitoring recipe detects drift. Database recipe adds migration gates. Code review recipe reviews manifests. Content recipe documents the model. However: (1) GitOps mandates that the Git repository is the SOLE source of truth — but during an active incident at 3 AM, the on-call engineer needs to kubectl scale a deployment immediately. This 'drift' is intentional and life-saving, but ArgoCD will automatically revert it within 3 minutes (its sync interval) unless the engineer ALSO pushes a Git commit while firefighting the incident, (2) the database migration safety recipe requires pre-deployment validation against a production-size test environment — but GitOps treats the entire cluster state as a single Git commit. A database migration that happens 'before' an application deployment can't be sequenced within a single Git commit, (3) the code review recipe requires manifest reviews, but ArgoCD auto-generates some manifests from Helm templates — the 'reviewed' Helm values produce DIFFERENT manifests depending on the Helm chart version, meaning a values.yaml review doesn't verify the actual deployed manifests, (4) the monitoring recipe detects 'configuration drift' but some drift is EXPECTED (HPA scaling replicas, VPA adjusting resources) — distinguishing intentional drift from accidental drift requires understanding Kubernetes semantics that the monitoring recipe doesn't cover. GitOps purists say 'no kubectl in production' — how do you handle emergencies?

tasks.title: Compose: Open Source Security Audit Where Every Finding Has Business Trade-offs
tasks.task: Security audit of an open-source dependency (the company's MOST CRITICAL runtime library — removing it would require a 6-month rewrite). Code review recipe audits critical paths. Database recipe analyzes data interaction patterns. API recipe assesses injection surfaces. Testing recipe designs fuzz testing. Content recipe produces audit report. However: (1) the code review found 3 'potential' vulnerabilities — but they require a specific call sequence that your application doesn't currently use. Reporting them would trigger your company's 'no unpatched critical CVEs' policy and force emergency remediation of theoretical risks, (2) the database interaction analysis reveals the library uses a custom query builder that doesn't use parameterized queries — the style_only_review anti-pattern would miss this because the code is syntactically correct, but it's semantically vulnerable, (3) the testing recipe's fuzz testing found a heap overflow at 10 million iterations — the library is used in a context where it processes at most 500 items. Is this a 'real' vulnerability or an academic finding?, (4) the audit report (content recipe) must be shared with the open-source maintainer for responsible disclosure — but publishing the findings before a fix exists creates a 0-day risk for all users of the library (including your competitors), (5) the library's maintainer is a single volunteer who has been unresponsive for 6 months. Audit thoroughly, report responsibly, and maintain business operations all at once.

tasks.title: Compose: Free Trial Optimization Where Growth Hacks Conflict With Ethical Design
tasks.task: Optimize SaaS free trial conversion to paid. Onboarding recipe designs adaptive guidance. Data analysis recipe analyzes trial behavior. Testing recipe designs A/B test framework. Monitoring recipe tracks conversion funnel. Content recipe documents experiments. However: (1) the data analysis recipe reveals that the highest-converting onboarding variation uses dark patterns: pre-selected annual billing, hidden cancellation, and 'confirm-shaming' language. The ethical design team objects but the growth team points to a 40% conversion lift, (2) the onboarding recipe says 'adaptive guidance by role' but A/B testing 4 trial variations × 5 roles = 20 experiment cells — with 300 new trials/month, each cell has only 15 users which is insufficient for statistical significance at the test runner's configured 95% confidence level. Running the experiment long enough requires 6+ months, (3) the monitoring recipe tracks the conversion funnel, but the metric the CEO cares about (trial-to-paid conversion %) doesn't distinguish between: healthy conversions (user found value), coerced conversions (user forgot to cancel), and churned-then-reconverted (user cancelled and came back) — optimizing the aggregate metric may increase coerced conversions, (4) the content recipe for experiment documentation must be accessible — but the experiment results contain customer behavioral data that raises GDPR purpose limitation questions (did users consent to their trial behavior being analyzed for conversion optimization?). Previous generic tutorial anti-pattern for trials caused 60% drop-off. Optimize conversion without dark patterns, bad statistics, or privacy violations.

tasks.title: Compose: Multi-Tenant Database Where Isolation Conflicts With Query Performance
tasks.task: Design multi-tenant database for a B2B SaaS platform. Database recipe designs schema with isolation. CI/CD recipe automates tenant provisioning. Monitoring recipe tracks per-tenant performance. API recipe manages tenants. However: (1) schema-per-tenant isolation (the database recipe's recommendation for regulated tenants) creates 500 schemas with identical structure — PostgreSQL's query planner degrades with 500+ schemas because pg_catalog grows proportionally, adding 200ms to the first query in each session, (2) the CI/CD recipe for tenant provisioning must handle the 'noisy neighbor' problem: creating a new tenant triggers schema creation + index creation + initial data seeding, which causes I/O spikes that degrade all existing tenants' performance for 30-60 seconds, (3) the monitoring recipe needs per-tenant performance tracking — but 500 tenants × 50 key metrics = 25,000 time series, exceeding the Prometheus cardinality limit on your monitoring cluster. The index_everything anti-pattern now applies to monitoring: instrumenting everything overwhelms the monitoring system, (4) the API recipe for tenant management needs to support self-service tenant creation via API — but the database recipe's schema-per-tenant approach requires DDL operations (CREATE SCHEMA, CREATE TABLE) which are expensive transactions that can't safely be triggered by API calls from untrusted users. A tenant-creation DDoS would overwhelm the database.

tasks.title: Compose: SOC Setup Where Alert Volume Overwhelms Human Operators
tasks.task: Set up a Security Operations Center monitoring system. Monitoring recipe designs threat dashboards. Incident response recipe creates alert-to-playbook mappings. Testing recipe designs validation. Content recipe writes runbooks. However: (1) the monitoring recipe wants comprehensive alert coverage — but the initial deployment with 'recommended' alert rules generates 2,400 alerts/day, of which 95% are false positives (normal business activity flagged as 'anomalous'). The alert_without_baseline anti-pattern at SECURITY SCALE — the SOC team of 3 analysts can investigate about 50 alerts/day maximum, (2) the incident response recipe says 'don't fix without root cause analysis' but for security incidents, CONTAINMENT (block the IP, disable the account) must happen IMMEDIATELY — root cause analysis happens AFTER containment, which contradicts the recipe, (3) the testing recipe says 'validate detection' — this means running ACTUAL ATTACK SIMULATIONS against production systems (red team), which the legal team requires pre-approval for, and the last red team exercise triggered a real incident response (Severity 1) because the SOC wasn't notified it was a test, (4) the runbook documentation contains sensitive information: specific detection rules, response thresholds, and known visibility gaps — making this accessible documentation means potential adversaries could read the exact conditions under which they WON'T be detected. Full SOC observability vs. operational security: how do you balance comprehensive documentation with adversary awareness?

tasks.title: Compose: Payment Gateway Where Provider Differences Break Consistent Abstractions
tasks.task: Design a payment gateway integration layer supporting Stripe, PayPal, and direct bank transfers (open banking API). API recipe demands consistent schemas. Code review recipe validates security. Database recipe designs transaction records. Testing recipe covers failure scenarios. Monitoring recipe tracks payment health. However: (1) Stripe and PayPal have fundamentally different payment lifecycle models — Stripe uses a 2-step capture model (authorize then capture) while PayPal Express uses a 1-step model with different refund semantics. A 'consistent API' that abstracts both must lose provider-specific functionality or leak the abstraction, (2) bank transfers are asynchronous (settlement in 1-5 business days) while card payments are synchronous (milliseconds) — a consistent 'payment status' API must represent states that exist for one provider but not another ('pending_settlement' for bank transfers, no equivalent for cards), (3) the code review recipe finds that the PayPal integration SDK hasn't been updated in 18 months and uses deprecated APIs — but PayPal's current SDK has a known thread-safety bug reported 4 months ago and unpatched. Use the deprecated but working SDK or the current but buggy one?, (4) the testing recipe wants failure scenario coverage — but Stripe's sandbox has reliable failure simulation while PayPal's sandbox intermittently fails to simulate ANY error states (their sandbox is notoriously unreliable), creating asymmetric test coverage that makes you confident about Stripe error handling but uncertain about PayPal, (5) the database recipe for transaction records must handle currency precision differences (JPY has 0 decimal places, BHD has 3, most currencies have 2) and the monitoring recipe's 'payment health' metric must account for provider-specific error rate baselines (bank transfers have a 5% normal failure rate vs cards at 0.5% — if you aggregate, the baseline is meaningless).

tasks.title: Compose: Platform Golden Path Where Every Team Has a Reason to Deviate
tasks.task: Implement the 'golden path' for a platform engineering team. All 8 skill recipes (CI/CD, code review, database, monitoring, onboarding, content, API, testing) must compose into a coherent, easy-to-adopt, hard-to-deviate-from standard. However: (1) the golden path must be SINGULAR (one way to do things) but the 50 consuming teams have legitimate reasons to deviate: Team A can't use the standard database migration tool because their regulatory auditor requires a specific approved tool, Team B's performance-critical service can't afford the standard monitoring library's 2ms overhead, Team C's ML pipeline doesn't fit the standard CI/CD stages, (2) every recipe implemented 'by the book' adds compliance overhead: standard code review (15 min), standard security scan (20 min), standard integration test (25 min), standard monitoring validation (10 min) = 70 minutes of mandatory gates per deploy. Teams deploying 10 times/day spend 700 minutes (11.6 hours) in gates, (3) the golden path documentation must be accessible (content recipe) but is 200+ pages — a new engineer can't absorb it during onboarding, meaning the onboarding recipe conflicts with the documentation completeness, (4) making the path 'hard to deviate from' means enforcement via CI/CD gates — but gates that BLOCK deployments create an adversarial relationship between platform team and product teams, leading to shadow CI/CD systems and workarounds that are LESS secure than the golden path they bypass. Previous anti-patterns from ALL 10 categories are relevant. How do you build a golden path that's genuinely golden (teams WANT to use it) rather than a golden cage (teams are FORCED to use it)?

tasks.title: Arch Review 01: Event-Driven Order Processing With Hidden Dual-Write
tasks.task: Review a food delivery order processing system: orders come in via REST API, are written to PostgreSQL (orders table), and then published to a Kafka topic for fulfillment. The team claims this is 'event-driven architecture.' However: the write to PostgreSQL and the publish to Kafka are in the same HTTP request handler but NOT in a transaction — if Kafka publish fails after the DB write succeeds, the order exists in the database but fulfillment never starts. The code uses a try/catch that logs the Kafka failure but returns HTTP 200 to the caller. Additionally, the orders table uses an auto-increment integer PK exposed in the API response, allowing competitors to estimate order volume by probing sequential IDs. The team added a Redis cache layer for 'read performance' but the cache invalidation is triggered by the Kafka consumer, creating a circular dependency: if Kafka is down, the cache serves stale data indefinitely because the invalidation event never fires. Identify ALL architectural issues including the dual-write problem, the information leakage, and the circular cache dependency.

tasks.title: Arch Review 02: Microservice Auth With Token Lifetime Contradiction
tasks.task: Review the authentication service for a healthcare portal. It uses JWT access tokens (15-min lifetime) with refresh tokens (30-day lifetime stored in PostgreSQL). The service issues tokens via /auth/token and validates via /auth/verify. Issues to find: (1) the /auth/verify endpoint performs a DATABASE LOOKUP on every verification call to check a token revocation list — this defeats the purpose of JWTs (which should be stateless verification via signature), creating a bottleneck of 500 DB queries/second during peak hours, (2) the refresh token rotation implementation has a race condition: if a client sends two concurrent requests with the same refresh token, both may succeed before the old token is marked as used, issuing two valid access tokens from one refresh, (3) the session invalidation on password change only revokes refresh tokens — existing 15-minute access tokens remain valid, meaning a compromised account stays accessible for up to 15 minutes after password reset, (4) the JWT signing key is a hardcoded string in the environment variable JWT_SECRET='healthcare-app-2024' shared across all service instances via a shared ConfigMap. Document ALL findings — you will need to reference the token lifecycle and DB-backed revocation issues in later reviews.

tasks.title: Arch Review 03: CQRS Implementation With Eventual Consistency Time Bomb
tasks.task: Review a financial reporting system using CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation). Commands write to a normalized PostgreSQL write model; a Debezium CDC pipeline replicates changes to an Elasticsearch read model. Issues to find: (1) the CDC pipeline has a configurable lag of 'up to 5 seconds' but the financial reconciliation query on the read model runs every 60 seconds — during the 5-second lag window, the reconciliation report may show stale balances, and the team 'solved' this by adding a 10-second sleep before reconciliation (a race condition disguised as a fix), (2) the write model uses SERIALIZABLE isolation for financial transactions but the CDC pipeline reads from the PostgreSQL WAL, which includes aborted transactions that were rolled back — the read model may contain phantom records from rolled-back transactions, (3) the Elasticsearch read model has no authentication (accessible on port 9200 to any pod in the cluster) because 'it's read-only data' — but the financial data includes customer account balances, (4) the entire CQRS pattern was adopted when the system had 10K records; it now has 50M records and the full Elasticsearch reindex (required after schema changes) takes 6 hours during which the read model is stale. Note: the CDC WAL-reading issue will interact with the dual-write problem from Review 01 in later system integrations.

tasks.title: Arch Review 04: API Gateway With Rate Limiting That Doesn't Actually Limit
tasks.task: Review the API gateway configuration for a multi-tenant SaaS platform. Kong Gateway sits in front of 12 microservices. Issues to find: (1) rate limiting is configured per-route, not per-tenant — a single tenant making 1000 req/min on /api/reports consumes the rate limit for ALL tenants on that route. Tenant A's burst blocks Tenant B's legitimate traffic, (2) the rate limiter uses Kong's in-memory 'local' strategy (not Redis-backed) across 4 gateway replicas — each replica independently tracks rate limits, so the effective limit is 4x the configured value (250 req/min × 4 replicas = 1000 actual), (3) the gateway terminates TLS but forwards traffic to backend services over HTTP (not mTLS) within the cluster — any pod can sniff inter-service traffic, (4) the gateway's health check endpoint /health returns 200 even when all backend services are down (it only checks the gateway process, not backend connectivity), meaning the load balancer considers the gateway healthy while it's returning 502s to all requests, (5) JWT validation at the gateway extracts user_id from the token payload but doesn't verify the signature — it delegates signature validation to individual services, but 3 of the 12 services skip validation because 'the gateway already checked it.' Document the per-replica rate limiting math — this specific issue resurfaces in Review 15's capacity planning.

tasks.title: Arch Review 05: Cache Architecture With Thundering Herd and Stale-While-Revalidate Deadlock
tasks.task: Review the caching architecture for a product catalog service. Three-layer cache: L1 (in-process Caffeine, 64MB, 5-min TTL), L2 (Redis cluster, 10GB, 30-min TTL), L3 (CDN edge cache, 1-hour TTL). Issues to find: (1) when L2 Redis cache expires for a popular product (100K views/day), all application instances simultaneously query the database (thundering herd) — the database connection pool (max 50 connections) is exhausted within 200ms, causing cascading failures, (2) the stale-while-revalidate implementation has a distributed lock (Redis SETNX) to prevent multiple revalidations, BUT: the lock TTL is 30 seconds while the database query for a complex product (with 200 variants) takes 45 seconds — the lock expires before the query completes, causing a SECOND revalidation attempt that also takes 45 seconds, creating an infinite loop of overlapping revalidations, (3) the L1 in-process cache is per-instance but cache invalidation events are broadcast via Redis pub/sub — when an instance is processing a request using L1 cached data and simultaneously receives an invalidation event, it may serve stale data for the remainder of that request lifecycle, (4) the CDN cache (L3) has no purge API integration — when a product price changes, L1 and L2 are invalidated within seconds but the CDN serves the old price for up to 1 hour. NOTE: The thundering herd pattern here will be the exact same issue you find in Review 12's search service, and the distributed lock TTL problem will recur in Review 22's distributed transaction coordinator.

tasks.title: Arch Review 06: Event Sourcing With Schema Evolution Landmine
tasks.task: Review the event sourcing implementation for an insurance claims system. Events are stored in a PostgreSQL events table with columns: aggregate_id, event_type, payload (JSONB), version, created_at. Issues to find: (1) the payload column stores raw JSON without any schema versioning — when the 'ClaimSubmitted' event schema changed 6 months ago (added 'adjustor_id' field, renamed 'amount' to 'claim_amount'), the replay logic crashes on old events because it expects the new field names. The team 'fixed' this with a try/catch that sets missing fields to null, silently losing data, (2) the event replay for aggregate reconstruction reads ALL events for an aggregate (no snapshots) — the oldest claim has 2,847 events spanning 3 years, and replay takes 12 seconds, (3) the version column is supposed to enforce optimistic concurrency but the INSERT trigger that auto-increments it has a race condition under concurrent command processing — two commands can receive the same version number, (4) the events table has no partitioning strategy and has grown to 180M rows — full table scans for projection rebuilds take 4 hours, (5) the 'ClaimDeleted' event was added for GDPR compliance but the original events (containing PII) remain in the event store because 'events are immutable' — the deletion event is a logical marker but the PII persists physically. The schema evolution problem compounds with the event format variations from Review 03's CDC issues.

tasks.title: Arch Review 07: Distributed Task Queue With Exactly-Once Illusion
tasks.task: Review the distributed task queue (Celery + RabbitMQ) for a document processing pipeline. Documents are uploaded, split into chunks, processed by ML models, and reassembled. Issues to find: (1) the system claims 'exactly-once processing' but the implementation is 'at-least-once with client-side deduplication' — the deduplication table uses a Redis SET with 24-hour TTL, meaning duplicate processing is possible for tasks retried after 24 hours, (2) the task chunking splits documents by page count but doesn't consider chunk processing ORDER — reassembly assumes chunks return in order, but Celery workers process them with variable latency, and 1 in 50 documents is reassembled with pages out of order, (3) the task visibility timeout is 30 minutes, but the ML model for large documents takes up to 45 minutes — RabbitMQ redelivers the task at 30 minutes to another worker, resulting in DUPLICATE processing and double billing for the ML API call, (4) the task failure retry uses exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, ...) with no maximum — after 10 retries, the backoff is 1024 seconds (17 minutes) and the user has already given up, (5) the RabbitMQ cluster uses mirrored queues (deprecated in RabbitMQ 3.13+) instead of quorum queues, risking message loss during network partitions. Note the exactly-once deduplication approach — Review 14 will reveal that the same deduplication approach is used for payment processing, where 'at-least-once with dedup' means potential double charges.

tasks.title: Arch Review 08: Database Sharding With Cross-Shard Query Explosion
tasks.task: Review the database sharding strategy for a social media platform. User data is sharded by user_id hash across 16 PostgreSQL instances. Issues to find: (1) the hash-based sharding works well for single-user queries but the 'feed' query (show posts from all followed users) requires SCATTER-GATHER across all 16 shards because followed users are on different shards — the feed query p99 is 4.2 seconds with 16 parallel DB calls, (2) the shard key (user_id) means a user's posts, comments, and likes are on the SAME shard — but a viral post with 500K likes spreads write load unevenly because 500K like-write operations hit the shard of the post author, not distributed across shards, (3) the resharding plan (adding shards 17-32) requires a full data migration because the hash function (user_id % 16) changes to (user_id % 32), moving approximately 50% of all data. The migration plan estimates 72 hours of dual-write mode, (4) cross-shard transactions (e.g., transferring virtual currency between users on different shards) use a 2-phase commit coordinator that has no timeout — a coordinator crash during phase 1 leaves transactions in PREPARED state permanently, requiring manual DBA intervention, (5) the connection pool per shard is 50 connections × 16 shards × 8 application instances = 6,400 total connections, approaching PostgreSQL's max_connections limit. The shard-explosion performance issues will compound when Review 17's analytics pipeline tries to run aggregation queries across all 16 shards.

tasks.title: Arch Review 09: Service Mesh With Observability Overhead Tax
tasks.task: Review the Istio service mesh deployment for a 30-microservice Kubernetes cluster. Issues to find: (1) the Envoy sidecar proxy adds 3ms latency per hop — for a request that crosses 7 services (common in the checkout flow), this adds 42ms of pure mesh overhead (7 hops × 2 sidecars per hop × 3ms), which is 40% of the 100ms latency SLO, (2) the mTLS configuration uses a single root CA for all services — if the CA is compromised, ALL mutual TLS is compromised simultaneously. There's no CA rotation plan, (3) the distributed tracing (Jaeger) samples 100% of traces in staging but only 1% in production to control storage costs — the 1% sampling means 99% of error traces are LOST, making production debugging nearly impossible for intermittent issues, (4) the Istio control plane (istiod) is a single replica — if it crashes, new sidecar configurations can't be distributed, and any service scaling event (new pod) will start WITHOUT a sidecar proxy (failing open), bypassing mTLS and authorization policies, (5) the service mesh's retry policy (3 retries, 1s timeout) is configured at the mesh level AND the application level — a failing request gets 3 mesh retries × 3 app retries = 9 actual attempts, amplifying load on an already-failing service by 9x. The 3ms-per-hop overhead directly impacts the latency budget reviewed in Review 04's gateway configuration, and the retry amplification pattern is the same thundering herd driver identified in Review 05.

tasks.title: Arch Review 10: Secrets Management With Bootstrap Chicken-and-Egg Problem
tasks.task: Review the secrets management architecture using HashiCorp Vault. Issues to find: (1) Vault unsealing requires 3 of 5 key shares, held by 5 engineers — but only 3 engineers are in the same timezone. After the last Vault restart (3 AM UTC), it took 90 minutes to gather 3 key holders for unsealing, during which NO service could authenticate, (2) the Vault agent running as a sidecar in Kubernetes pods caches secrets in a tmpfs volume — but if the agent's Vault token expires (24-hour TTL) and Vault is unreachable, the cached secret persists until the pod restarts. A secret rotation event at Vault isn't reflected in running pods until they restart or the agent successfully renews, (3) database credentials are dynamically generated by Vault with a 1-hour TTL — but the application's connection pool holds connections created with THE OLD credential. When the credential expires at Vault, existing DB connections still work (PostgreSQL doesn't re-authenticate active connections), but new connections fail. The app sees sporadic 'authentication failed' errors for new connections while old connections work fine, creating a confusing diagnostic scenario, (4) the bootstrap problem: Kubernetes pods need Vault to get secrets, but the Vault auth method needs the Kubernetes service account token, which is mounted by the Kubernetes API server, which needs TLS certs, which are managed by cert-manager, which needs its own Vault credentials. If Vault is down, nothing can start. The 1-hour credential TTL interacts with the connection pool issues identified in Review 08 (connection pool per shard × credential rotation = potential authentication storms across all shards simultaneously).

tasks.title: Arch Review 11: Notification Service Cross-Referencing Previous Findings
tasks.task: Review the notification service that sends emails, SMS, and push notifications. It consumes events from the same Kafka topic reviewed in Review 01. Issues to find: (1) the notification service suffers from the SAME dual-write problem as Review 01 — it reads from Kafka, marks the notification as 'sent' in its own PostgreSQL database, AND calls the email/SMS provider API, without transactional guarantees across these three operations. If the provider API call succeeds but the DB write fails, the notification is sent but not tracked, leading to re-sends on retry, (2) the Kafka consumer uses 'auto-commit' offsets every 5 seconds — if the service crashes between processing a message and the next auto-commit, the message is reprocessed on restart. Combined with the exactly-once illusion from Review 07, this means notifications can be duplicated, (3) the SMS provider has a rate limit of 100 messages/second, but the notification service has no rate limiting — during a marketing campaign (50K notifications in 1 minute), the service fires 833 msg/sec, gets rate-limited by the provider, and the resulting 429 responses trigger Kafka consumer backpressure that affects ALL Kafka consumers in the consumer group (including non-notification consumers sharing the group), (4) the push notification token storage table has user_id + device_token but no index on device_token — querying for 'which user owns this device token' (needed for token invalidation) requires a full table scan of 5M rows. This is the same missing-index-for-reverse-lookups issue as the JWT revocation lookup in Review 02.

tasks.title: Arch Review 12: Search Service Revisiting Cache Thundering Herd
tasks.task: Review the Elasticsearch-based search service for the product catalog. Issues to find: (1) the exact same thundering herd pattern from Review 05 exists here: when the Elasticsearch query cache expires, all search requests hit ES simultaneously. But unlike Review 05 where the L2 Redis provided a buffer, the search service has NO intermediate cache — it goes directly from application to Elasticsearch, (2) the search index has 12 shards across 3 nodes — but the product catalog has only 500K documents. Over-sharding creates overhead: each search query hits all 12 shards even for simple keyword matches, and the shard-level overhead (open file handles, thread pools, memory) exceeds the benefit for this data volume, (3) the search relevance scoring uses a custom scoring script that calls an external ML model via HTTP for EVERY search result on EVERY query — for a query returning 100 results, this is 100 HTTP calls to the ML service per search request, adding 2-3 seconds of latency, (4) the index mapping uses 'dynamic: true' meaning any new field in the document is automatically indexed — a bug in the product import pipeline added a 50KB 'debug_trace' field to every document, inflating the index from 2GB to 25GB before anyone noticed, (5) the reindex process (needed for mapping changes) uses the same Elasticsearch cluster for source and destination, competing with live queries for I/O. This is the CDC/consistency issue from Review 03 manifesting in a different context — changes to the product database take up to 10 minutes to appear in search.

tasks.title: Arch Review 13: File Storage Service With Lifecycle Policy Gaps
tasks.task: Review the file storage service (S3 + CloudFront). Issues to find: (1) uploaded files are stored with predictable object keys (/{tenant_id}/{file_id}.pdf) — combined with the sequential ID leakage from Review 01, an attacker who knows the tenant_id can enumerate file_ids because they're UUIDv1 (time-based, predictable), not UUIDv4 (random), (2) the CloudFront distribution has no signed URLs — any file URL generated for authenticated user A can be shared with unauthenticated user B, who can access it for 24 hours (the CloudFront cache TTL), (3) the S3 lifecycle policy deletes files after 90 days, but the legal team's data retention policy requires 7 years for compliance documents — the lifecycle policy applies to the ENTIRE bucket with no differentiation between ephemeral uploads and compliance documents, (4) the virus scanning Lambda function runs AFTER the file is stored in S3 and the presigned upload URL is returned to the user — for a 3-second window, an infected file is accessible via its S3 URL before scanning completes. If Review 01's notification system sends a 'file uploaded' event immediately, downstream systems may process the infected file before scanning, (5) the file deduplication logic uses MD5 hash comparison — MD5 collisions are well-known, and two different files with the same MD5 hash would be treated as identical, potentially serving the wrong content. The predictable-key vulnerability compounds with the JWT issues from Review 02 (if JWT signature isn't validated at gateway, any valid token can access any tenant's files).

tasks.title: Arch Review 14: Payment Processing Revisiting Exactly-Once and Dual-Write
tasks.task: Review the payment processing service. It uses Stripe as the payment provider. Issues to find: (1) the SAME at-least-once-with-dedup approach from Review 07 is used here: a Redis SET with 24-hour TTL for idempotency keys. After 24 hours, retrying a payment creates a SECOND charge. For a $10K enterprise payment that gets retried after a weekend (48 hours), the customer is charged twice because the idempotency key expired, (2) the dual-write problem from Review 01 manifests critically here: the service calls Stripe API (charge the card), then writes the transaction to the local database. If the DB write fails after Stripe succeeds, the payment is collected but not recorded — revenue is lost from the accounting system, (3) the payment webhook handler (Stripe→service callback for async events like chargebacks) has no signature verification — any POST request to /webhooks/stripe with a well-formatted body is trusted, allowing fake chargeback events that can credit arbitrary amounts back to user accounts, (4) the service stores full card numbers in a 'payment_attempts' logging table 'for debugging' — this is a PCI-DSS violation, creating compliance risk for the entire organization, (5) the retry logic for failed Stripe API calls doesn't check whether the failure was BEFORE or AFTER the charge was created — retrying an HTTP timeout (where Stripe may have processed the charge but the response was lost) can result in double charges. Cross-reference: this is the most critical manifestation of the dual-write and exactly-once problems identified in Reviews 01 and 07.
