tasks.title: Arch Review 15: Capacity Planning Compounding Previous Rate Limit Findings
tasks.task: Review the capacity planning model for the platform. The current projections are based on linear extrapolation of the last 6 months of traffic data. Issues to find: (1) the effective rate limit is 4x the configured value (from Review 04's per-replica rate limiting bug) — capacity planning is based on the CONFIGURED limit, not the ACTUAL limit. If the bug is ever fixed, capacity would need to be 4x higher because clients are currently allowed 4x more traffic than intended, (2) the connection pool math from Review 08 (6,400 total connections) appears in capacity planning as '6,400 connections with room to grow' — but the plan to add 16 more shards would DOUBLE this to 12,800 connections, exceeding PostgreSQL's default max_connections by 12× and requiring pgBouncer, which isn't in the capacity plan, (3) the cache thundering herd from Reviews 05 and 12 means that capacity for database queries is NOT the steady-state load but the BURST load during cache misses — the capacity plan uses average QPS instead of peak burst QPS, underestimating required DB capacity by 10×, (4) the Istio sidecar proxy's 3ms overhead from Review 09 is not reflected in the capacity plan's latency budget — the plan allocates 100ms total but doesn't subtract the 42ms mesh overhead for 7-hop paths, leaving only 58ms for actual computation, (5) the Vault credential rotation from Review 10 creates authentication storms every hour across all shards — the capacity plan doesn't model these periodic spikes. WITHOUT memory of the specific findings from Reviews 01, 04, 05, 08, 09, 10, and 12, this capacity review cannot identify the compound errors.

tasks.title: Arch Review 16: CI/CD Pipeline Interacting With Secrets and Auth Findings
tasks.task: Review the CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions → Docker build → ArgoCD deploy). Issues to find: (1) the Dockerfiles use 'latest' tags for base images — a breaking change in the base image on Tuesday caused all builds to fail, but the team couldn't reproduce locally because their local Docker cache had the old 'latest', (2) the GitHub Actions workflows have secrets (API keys, push tokens) stored as repository secrets, but 15 of the 40 workflows print environment variables in debug mode — running with ACTIONS_STEP_DEBUG=true exposes all secrets in plaintext in build logs, (3) ArgoCD syncs from the 'main' branch with auto-sync enabled — any commit to main (including documentation changes) triggers a full production deployment. There's no staging environment in the pipeline, (4) the Docker images are pushed to ECR WITHOUT vulnerability scanning (the same deploy-without-security-scan pattern from Track B) — and the base images haven't been updated in 8 months, accumulating 47 known CVEs, (5) the ArgoCD service account has cluster-admin privileges 'for convenience' — a compromise of ArgoCD gives full control of the entire Kubernetes cluster. Cross-reference: the JWT secret from Review 02 (JWT_SECRET='healthcare-app-2024') is stored as a GitHub Actions secret and is visible in debug logs (issue #2). The Vault bootstrapping problem from Review 10 means the CI/CD pipeline can't deploy Vault itself without the bootstrap credentials that Vault manages.

tasks.title: Arch Review 17: Analytics Pipeline Compounding Sharding and Consistency Issues
tasks.task: Review the analytics pipeline (Kafka → Spark Structured Streaming → Snowflake). Issues to find: (1) the Spark job reads from the SAME Kafka topics as the notification service (Review 11) using a separate consumer group — but when Review 11's consumer falls behind (due to SMS rate limiting backpressure), the analytics consumer's lag metric looks healthy even though BOTH are reading from a topic with production delays, (2) the scatter-gather query problem from Review 08 is amplified here: the analytics pipeline needs to join data from all 16 database shards for aggregation. It does this via 16 parallel JDBC connections in Spark, which consumes 16 × 4 = 64 connections per Spark executor × 20 executors = 1,280 connections — adding to the already-strained 6,400 connection count from Review 08, (3) the pipeline uses the Elasticsearch read model from Review 03 for enrichment lookups — but Review 03 identified WAL replication lag and potential phantom records, meaning the analytics pipeline may join against stale or invalid enrichment data, (4) the Snowflake staging tables use 'CREATE OR REPLACE' which drops and recreates the table on every load — if two pipeline runs overlap, the second DROPS the table the first created, losing the first batch's data, (5) the CDC pipeline from Review 03 feeds BOTH the Elasticsearch read model AND the analytics pipeline — a schema change in the CDC events requires coordinated updates to both consumers, but they're owned by different teams. Document how FIVE previous review findings compound in this single analytics pipeline.

tasks.title: Arch Review 18: Mobile BFF With Session State Inconsistency Across Reviews
tasks.task: Review the Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) for the mobile app. Issues to find: (1) the BFF aggregates responses from 5 microservices into a single mobile-optimized payload — but it calls them SEQUENTIALLY (not in parallel), meaning mobile response time = sum of 5 service latencies + 5 × Istio mesh overhead (from Review 09's 3ms/hop finding) = service latency + 30ms extra, (2) the BFF maintains a session store in Redis for mobile-specific state (cart, recently viewed) — but this is a SEPARATE Redis from the cache Redis in Review 05, meaning two Redis clusters to maintain. The BFF Redis uses eviction policy 'allkeys-lru' meaning session data can be evicted under memory pressure, randomly logging out mobile users, (3) the BFF uses the same JWT validation approach as the gateway (Review 04) — it extracts user_id from the JWT WITHOUT signature verification, trusting the gateway to have verified. But Review 04 found that 3 backend services skip verification; the BFF is now a 4th service that skips verification, (4) the mobile app sends device tokens for push notifications to the BFF, which writes them to the same unindexed table from Review 11 (notification service) — the full table scan problem is now triggered by BOTH the notification service and the BFF, (5) the BFF has a 10-second timeout but the payment service (Review 14) has a 30-second timeout for Stripe API calls — if a mobile user initiates a payment, the BFF times out at 10 seconds while the payment is still processing. The user sees an error, retries, and gets double-charged (compounding Review 14's payment retry issue).

tasks.title: Arch Review 19: Data Lake Governance With PII Leaking From Multiple Sources
tasks.task: Review the data lake architecture (S3 data lake + Glue catalog + Athena queries). Issues to find: (1) the data lake ingests data from 6 sources including the analytics pipeline (Review 17), the notification logs (Review 11), and the payment audit logs (Review 14). The payment audit logs contain the PCI-DSS violations identified in Review 14 (full card numbers in 'payment_attempts' logs) — these card numbers are now replicated to the data lake, spreading the PCI violation to a second storage system, (2) the Glue catalog has no column-level access control — any analyst with Athena access can query the full card numbers, the healthcare JWT secrets (if any auth logs are ingested from Review 02's system), and the file storage paths (Review 13's predictable S3 keys), (3) the data lake's 'raw' zone stores data as-is with no transformation — this means the CDC phantom records from Review 03 (events from rolled-back transactions) are permanently stored in the data lake as if they were real, (4) the GDPR right-to-erasure (relevant from Review 06's event sourcing PII issue) applies to the data lake too — but S3 objects are immutable and the data lake has no mechanism to find and delete all records for a specific user across 6 source tables and 3 years of partitioned data, (5) the data lake partitions by date (yyyy/mm/dd prefixes) — querying across partitions for regulatory reports requires scanning ALL partitions, and 3 years of daily partitions = 1,095 partition scans per query.

tasks.title: Arch Review 20: Tenant Isolation Compounding Gateway, Auth, and Database Findings
tasks.task: Review the multi-tenant isolation model. Issues to find: (1) tenant isolation is enforced at the APPLICATION layer (WHERE tenant_id = ?) but NOT at the database layer — a SQL injection in any service bypasses all tenant isolation. This compounds with Review 14's missing webhook signature verification: a fake webhook could trigger cross-tenant data access if the webhook handler doesn't filter by tenant_id, (2) the API gateway rate limiting from Review 04 (per-route, not per-tenant) means one tenant can DoS another. The capacity plan from Review 15 doesn't account for per-tenant resource guarantees, (3) the shared Redis cluster from Review 05's caching architecture stores cache entries for ALL tenants with keys like 'product:{id}' — but product IDs are globally unique, not tenant-scoped. If Tenant A and Tenant B happen to have a product with the same external SKU, the cache may serve Tenant A's data to Tenant B, (4) the file storage paths from Review 13 (/{tenant_id}/{file_id}.pdf) provide tenant isolation at the S3 level, BUT the CloudFront distribution serves all tenants from the same CDN — a URL generated for Tenant A's file doesn't include any tenant-scoped authorization, (5) the database sharding from Review 08 shards by user_id, NOT by tenant_id — users from different tenants co-exist on the same shard, meaning a noisy tenant's heavy queries slow down other tenants sharing the same shard. WITHOUT memory of the specific isolation gaps found in Reviews 02, 04, 05, 08, 13, and 14, this review cannot identify the compound cross-tenant attack surface.

tasks.title: Arch Review 21: Disaster Recovery With Cross-System Dependencies
tasks.task: Review the disaster recovery plan. Issues to find: (1) the DR plan orders recovery as: (a) database, (b) applications, (c) integrations. But Review 10's Vault bootstrap problem means the applications can't start without Vault, Vault needs the database (for its backend), and the database needs Vault (for credential rotation). The circular dependency was never acknowledged in the DR plan, (2) the RTO is 4 hours, but the Elasticsearch reindex from Review 03 takes 6 hours — the search functionality won't be available within the RTO window, (3) the DR site is in a different region where the S3 bucket replication (Review 13's file storage) has a 15-minute lag — files uploaded in the last 15 minutes before a disaster are lost, (4) the DR plan assumes the JWT_SECRET from Review 02 is the same in both regions, but it's hardcoded in each region's ConfigMap independently — after the last secret rotation, the primary region has JWT_SECRET='healthcare-app-2024' but the DR region still has JWT_SECRET='healthcare-app-2023'. All existing sessions fail after failover, (5) the Kafka topics (from Reviews 01, 07, 11, 17) use replication factor 3 within a region but NO cross-region replication — failover loses ALL in-flight events, including unprocessed payment events from Review 14 (meaning payments that were charged at Stripe but not recorded locally are now PERMANENTLY lost). Document how findings from Reviews 01, 02, 03, 07, 10, 13, 14, and 17 compound into a DR plan with 8 critical gaps.

tasks.title: Arch Review 22: Distributed Transaction Coordinator Revisiting Lock and Timeout Issues
tasks.task: Review the distributed transaction coordinator (Saga pattern implementation). Issues to find: (1) the saga coordinator uses the SAME distributed lock approach from Review 05's cache (Redis SETNX with TTL) — and has the SAME bug: the lock TTL (30 seconds) is shorter than the longest saga step (payment processing via Review 14's Stripe integration, which can take up to 45 seconds for 3D Secure), causing concurrent saga instances for the same order, (2) the compensating transactions for saga rollback don't handle idempotency — if the 'cancel payment' compensation is retried (due to Review 07's exactly-once illusion), the refund is issued TWICE, (3) the saga state is stored in the same PostgreSQL instance as the order data (Review 01's system) — but there's no mechanism to clean up 'stuck' sagas where the coordinator crashed mid-execution. Stuck sagas hold database locks (not the distributed locks, but actual row-level advisory locks) that block other transactions, (4) the saga timeout is 5 minutes, but the total time for a saga with 6 steps × potential retries × exponential backoff (Review 07's retry pattern) can exceed 30 minutes — the saga times out and triggers compensation WHILE the slow step is still running, leading to a race between the step completing successfully and the compensation reversing it, (5) the coordinator logs saga state transitions to Kafka (using Review 01's same publish-without-transaction pattern), meaning saga state in PostgreSQL and saga events in Kafka can diverge.

tasks.title: Arch Review 23: Monitoring and Alerting Compounding All Observability Gaps
tasks.task: Review the monitoring and alerting configuration (Prometheus + Grafana + PagerDuty). Issues to find: (1) Prometheus scrapes every 15 seconds but the Istio sidecar metrics (Review 09) have a 30-second aggregation window — the Prometheus scrape interval is FASTER than the metric aggregation, meaning every other scrape gets stale data, creating sawtooth graphs that confuse on-call engineers, (2) the alerting rules reference metrics that Review 04's API gateway emits, but the per-replica rate limiting bug means the 'rate_limited_requests' metric undercounts by 4x — the alert threshold was set based on the incorrect metric, meaning the alert fires at 250 req/min (per replica) when the actual platform-wide rate is 1000 req/min, (3) the PagerDuty integration uses email notifications, not the Events API v2 — alert deduplication doesn't work, and a single flapping service generates 50 pages in 10 minutes. The Vault credential rotation storm from Review 10 (every hour) triggers authentication-failure alerts that are FALSE POSITIVES, adding 24 fake alerts/day, (4) Grafana dashboards show metrics from 5 data sources (Prometheus, Elasticsearch from Review 03, CloudWatch for S3 from Review 13, Jaeger for traces from Review 09, and custom application metrics) — but there's no correlation ID linking a request across all 5 data sources, meaning debugging requires manual correlation across 5 dashboards, (5) the disk usage alert for the PostgreSQL events table (Review 06) is set at 80% — but the table grows 5GB/day and the disk is 500GB, meaning the alert fires 10 days before the disk is full, which SEEMS like adequate warning but the table compaction job that frees space takes 12 hours during which write performance degrades.

tasks.title: Arch Review 24: API Versioning Strategy Compounding Backward Compatibility Gaps
tasks.task: Review the API versioning strategy. The platform uses URL-path versioning (/v1/, /v2/) with 3 active versions. Issues to find: (1) v1 was designed before the multi-tenant isolation reviewed in Review 20 — v1 endpoints don't include tenant_id scoping, meaning legacy v1 consumers bypass tenant isolation entirely. The plan to 'deprecate v1' has been delayed 18 months because the largest customer (40% of revenue) refuses to upgrade, (2) v2 introduced breaking changes to the payment webhook format (Review 14) — but the webhook delivery system doesn't version-tag webhook payloads. Consumers who expect v1 webhooks but see v2 payloads silently drop payment notifications, causing unrecorded charges, (3) the API gateway (Review 04) routes versions by URL prefix, but v1 and v2 of the SAME resource return DIFFERENT response schemas — the CDN (from Review 13's CloudFront) caches based on URL only, not on Accept headers or API version negotiation, meaning cached v1 responses can be served to v2 clients and vice versa, (4) the GraphQL endpoint (added in v3) coexists with REST v1 and v2 — but the GraphQL schema was auto-generated from the database schema (including the internal fields from Review 03's CQRS implementation like _soft_deleted and _partition_key), exposing internal implementation details through the API, (5) the SDK (client libraries in Python, Java, Node) is version-pinned to v2 — customers on v1 use raw HTTP calls, bypassing all SDK-level validation and retry logic, increasing the likelihood of the payment double-charge scenario from Reviews 07 and 14.

tasks.title: Arch Review 25: Mid-Series Comprehensive Architecture Assessment
tasks.task: Conduct a mid-series comprehensive architecture assessment that synthesizes findings from ALL previous 24 reviews into a risk-prioritized roadmap. This review must: (1) Categorize all findings by severity: CRITICAL (active data loss or security breach risk), HIGH (correctness issues that will eventually cause incidents), MEDIUM (performance and reliability risks), LOW (technical debt that complicates operations). CRITICAL findings include: dual-write data loss (Reviews 01, 14, 22), payment double-charge (Reviews 07, 14, 18), PCI violations (Reviews 14, 19), missing JWT verification chain (Reviews 02, 04, 18), cross-tenant data leakage (Reviews 05, 13, 20), PII in immutable stores (Reviews 06, 19), and DR plan gaps (Review 21), (2) Identify finding INTERACTIONS where fixing one issue worsens another: fixing Review 04's rate limiting exposes Review 15's capacity gap; fixing Review 10's Vault unseal time requires automation that introduces Review 16's CI/CD secret exposure risk; fixing Review 08's shard count worsens Review 17's connection count, (3) Propose a sequenced remediation plan that respects dependencies: e.g., fix Review 02's JWT verification before Review 20's tenant isolation (because tenant isolation without auth is meaningless), fix Review 10's Vault bootstrap before Review 16's CI/CD secrets, (4) Calculate the compound risk: how many of the 24 review findings can be chained together for a single attack vector? (Hint: Reviews 02→04→13→20 chain into: forge JWT → bypass gateway auth → enumerate S3 file keys → access other tenants' files.) WITHOUT memory of the specific technical details from ALL 24 previous reviews, this assessment cannot identify the interactions, chains, or priorities.

tasks.title: Arch Review 26: Compliance Audit Trail With Immutability Contradictions
tasks.task: Review the audit trail system for regulatory compliance (SOC 2, HIPAA). Issues to find: (1) audit events are stored in the SAME PostgreSQL instance as application data (Review 01's system) — a database administrator (DBA) can modify audit records, violating the immutability requirement that SOC 2 mandates, (2) the audit trail captures API request/response payloads for accountability — but Review 14's payment processing stores full card numbers in attempts logs, meaning the audit trail also captures PCI data (compounding the violation identified in Review 19's data lake), (3) the audit trail retention is 7 years (regulatory requirement) but the PostgreSQL database from Review 08 is sharded by user_id — when a user is deleted (GDPR right-to-erasure from Review 06), their shard still contains the audit records that must be retained for 7 years while the personal data must be deleted, creating a direct conflict between GDPR and SOC 2, (4) the audit system uses the same Kafka pipeline from Review 01 for async event capture — Review 01's dual-write problem means audit events can be lost when Kafka publish fails, creating regulatory gaps, (5) the mid-series assessment in Review 25 identified the JWT→gateway→file chain as a compound attack vector — but the audit trail doesn't log FAILED authentication attempts (only successful API calls), meaning a brute-force attack exploiting Reviews 02 and 04's auth gaps leaves no audit trace.

tasks.title: Arch Review 27: Feature Flag System With Stale Evaluation Risks
tasks.task: Review the feature flag system (LaunchDarkly self-hosted). Issues to find: (1) feature flag evaluations are cached in the L1 in-process cache from Review 05 (5-min TTL) — a 'kill switch' flag toggled during an incident takes up to 5 minutes to propagate to all instances, during which the buggy code path continues executing, (2) the feature flag SDK stores a local fallback configuration file — if LaunchDarkly's streaming connection drops for 5+ minutes, the SDK falls back to the FILE, which was last updated during deployment. Any flags toggled AFTER deployment aren't in the file, meaning the fallback state diverges from the current state, (3) the flag targeting rules reference user attributes (plan_type, tenant_id) — but the BFF from Review 18 passes user attributes extracted from the unverified JWT (Reviews 02, 04, 18's chain of unverified JWTs). A forged JWT with premium plan_type gets premium features, (4) the CI/CD pipeline (Review 16) creates feature flags in LaunchDarkly during deployment — but there's no cleanup: 340 feature flags exist and only 50 are active. The stale 290 flags reference deprecated code paths, some of which have known vulnerabilities, and re-enabling them accidentally would reintroduce fixed bugs, (5) flag changes aren't subject to the code review process from Review 16's CI/CD — a product manager can toggle a flag that routes traffic to an unreviewed code path, bypassing the entire review and security scanning pipeline.

tasks.title: Arch Review 28: GraphQL Federation With N+1 and Authorization Gaps
tasks.task: Review the Apollo GraphQL Federation gateway that stitches subgraphs from 8 microservices. Issues to find: (1) the federation gateway enables queries that cross subgraph boundaries, creating N+1 patterns: query { users { orders { products { reviews } } } } fetches 1 users query + N order queries + N×M product queries + N×M×K review queries — a single GraphQL query generates O(N³) downstream requests, (2) authorization is enforced at the subgraph level, not the gateway — the gateway stitches responses from subgraphs, but if the 'reviews' subgraph doesn't enforce the same tenant isolation as the 'users' subgraph (Review 20's tenant isolation gap), the federated response leaks cross-tenant review data, (3) the gateway has a query depth limit of 10, but Review 24's API versioning means the REST-to-GraphQL adapter translates v1 REST calls into GraphQL queries with unpredictable depth, sometimes exceeding the limit and failing, (4) the subgraph schemas were auto-generated (same as Review 24's issue), exposing internal database fields through the federated schema, (5) the gateway's query plan cache (in-memory, per-instance) consumes 2GB RAM per pod — with 8 gateway pods, that's 16GB of cached query plans, but the cache hit rate is only 30% because the long-tail of unique queries overwhelms the cache. The N+1 amplification combined with Review 09's 3ms-per-hop mesh overhead means a deep query generates thousands of sidecar-proxied requests, each adding 3ms.

tasks.title: Arch Review 29: Background Job Scheduler With Priority Inversion
tasks.task: Review the background job scheduler (Sidekiq with Redis). Issues to find: (1) all background jobs (from low-priority report generation to high-priority payment reconciliation) share the SAME Redis instance as Review 05's cache — when the Redis cluster has memory pressure, cache eviction (Review 05's LRU policy) can evict Sidekiq's job queue data, SILENTLY DROPPING queued jobs, (2) the job priority system uses numeric priorities 1-10, but 80% of jobs are set to priority 5 ('medium') because teams defaulted to the middle value — priority is meaningless when everything is the same priority, and the payment reconciliation jobs (CRITICAL, from Review 14's system) compete equally with marketing email batch jobs, (3) a long-running analytics job (from Review 17's pipeline) blocks a Sidekiq worker for 45 minutes — with only 25 workers total, a batch of 25 analytics jobs monopolizes ALL workers, causing priority inversion where urgent payment jobs wait behind long analytics jobs, (4) the retry logic is the same exponential backoff from Review 07 (no maximum) — a job that depends on Review 10's Vault for credentials and Vault is temporarily unreachable will retry with increasing delays, eventually backing off to 17-minute intervals. If Vault comes back after 5 minutes, the job waits an additional 12 minutes unnecessarily, (5) the scheduler uses cron-style triggers but doesn't handle clock skew — if the scheduler pod's clock drifts (Kubernetes isn't NTP-synced in this cluster), jobs run early or late, and the hourly Vault credential rotation from Review 10 may misalign with the Sidekiq job that uses those credentials.

tasks.title: Arch Review 30: WebSocket Infrastructure With Connection State Explosion
tasks.task: Review the WebSocket infrastructure for real-time features (live dashboard updates, chat, notifications). Issues to find: (1) the WebSocket server maintains in-memory connection state (10K concurrent connections, each with user session data) — this state is NOT replicated, so a rolling deployment (Review 16's ArgoCD) terminates all 10K connections simultaneously, causing a thundering reconnection herd (same pattern as Review 05's cache thundering herd but for connections), (2) the WebSocket authentication uses the same JWT approach from Reviews 02, 04, 18 — the token is validated ONCE at connection establishment but never re-validated during the connection lifetime. A revoked token continues to receive real-time updates for up to 24 hours (the connection keep-alive duration), (3) the WebSocket server broadcasts events by iterating over all connections and sending to each — for a broadcast to 10K connections, this takes 3 seconds, during which new incoming messages are buffered (head-of-line blocking). The notification service from Review 11 pushes updates through WebSockets, meaning SMS rate-limiting backpressure from Review 11 causes WebSocket broadcast delays, (4) the WebSocket cluster uses Redis pub/sub for cross-server event distribution — but this is the SAME Redis as Review 05's cache and Review 29's Sidekiq, meaning memory pressure from any of the three systems affects all three, (5) connection count metrics aren't included in the capacity plan from Review 15 — the plan accounts for HTTP connections but not the 10K persistent WebSocket connections that each consume a file descriptor, a thread, and memory for session state.

tasks.title: Arch Review 31: Database Migration Framework With Schema Drift
tasks.task: Review the database migration framework (Flyway). Issues to find: (1) Flyway migrations run against the PRIMARY PostgreSQL instance and rely on replication to propagate to the 16 shards from Review 08 — but the migrations include CREATE INDEX statements that take 45 minutes on large tables. The primary completes the migration, Flyway marks it as 'applied', and the application deploys assuming the new index exists — but 15 of 16 shards are still building the index, meaning queries that depend on the new index FAIL on those shards, (2) the migration naming convention (V1_001__, V1_002__) was abandoned in favor of timestamps (V20250101__) midway through the project — Flyway sorts migrations lexicographically, and V1_xxx comes BEFORE V20250101__, meaning new timestamp-based migrations run AFTER the old numbered ones even if they should run first, (3) the rollback strategy is 'run a compensating migration' (not Flyway's undo feature) — but the compensating migrations are untested. Review 06's event sourcing schema change broke replay; the compensating migration to revert it would drop the new column, losing data written since the migration, (4) the migration that added tenant isolation columns (Review 20's WHERE tenant_id = ?) was a data migration (UPDATE all rows with tenant_id) — on shards with 50M rows, this UPDATE takes 4 hours, during which the shard is effectively write-locked, (5) the Flyway configuration uses hardcoded database credentials, not Vault-managed credentials from Review 10 — a credential rotation event invalidates Flyway's connection mid-migration, potentially leaving the schema in a partially-migrated state.

tasks.title: Arch Review 32: Rate Limiting Redesign Incorporating Gateway and Capacity Findings
tasks.task: Review a proposed rate limiting redesign that attempts to fix Review 04's per-replica bug. Issues to find: (1) the proposed fix moves rate limiting to a centralized Redis counter — but this introduces the SAME thundering herd risk from Review 05: all gateway replicas now compete for a single Redis key, and at 10K req/sec, the Redis key receives 10K INCR operations/second, which is near Redis's single-key throughput limit, (2) the centralized rate limiter needs to differentiate by tenant (fixing Review 04's per-route-not-per-tenant bug) — but tenant identification requires JWT parsing, and Review 04 found the gateway doesn't verify JWT signatures. The rate limiter would now trust the tenant_id from an unverified JWT, meaning an attacker can forge a JWT with a different tenant_id to bypass their rate limit, (3) the fix changes the effective limit from 4x the configured value (Review 04) to 1x — but Review 15's capacity plan is based on 4x. Fixing rate limiting correctly will cause legitimate traffic to be rate-limited, creating a user-facing outage, (4) the proposed sliding window algorithm uses Redis sorted sets, which consume more memory than simple counters — with 500 tenants × 100 rate limit rules × sliding window data, the memory consumption in the shared Redis cluster (from Reviews 05, 29, 30) adds pressure to an already contested resource, (5) the rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining) are returned by the gateway but cached by the CDN from Review 13's CloudFront — cached rate limit headers show STALE remaining counts, confusing client-side retry logic.

tasks.title: Arch Review 33: Data Encryption Strategy With Key Management Gaps
tasks.task: Review the encryption-at-rest and in-transit strategy. Issues to find: (1) database encryption at rest uses PostgreSQL's native TDE (Transparent Data Encryption) — but the encryption key is derived from a passphrase stored in the SAME ConfigMap as the JWT secret from Review 02. A ConfigMap compromise exposes BOTH the JWT signing key AND the database encryption passphrase, (2) the field-level encryption for PII (using application-level AES-256) stores the encryption key in Vault (Review 10) — but Review 10's bootstrap problem means that if Vault is unavailable, the application can't decrypt ANY PII fields, turning a Vault outage into a total PII data accessibility outage, (3) in-transit encryption relies on Istio's mTLS from Review 09 — but Review 09 found that if istiod crashes, new pods start WITHOUT sidecar proxies, transmitting data in plaintext. The encryption strategy doesn't account for this mTLS gap, (4) the file storage encryption from Review 13 uses S3 SSE-S3 (Amazon-managed keys) — this meets storage encryption requirements but AWS manages the key, meaning a legal subpoena to AWS could theoretically compel decryption without the customer's knowledge, violating some healthcare compliance requirements (relevant to Review 02's healthcare context), (5) the Kafka topics from Reviews 01, 07, 11, 17 transmit data in PLAINTEXT within the cluster — Kafka's inter-broker communication and client-broker communication are unencrypted, meaning any pod in the cluster can consume sensitive data from Kafka topics, including the payment events from Review 14.

tasks.title: Arch Review 34: Incident Postmortem Analysis Revealing Systemic Patterns
tasks.task: Review the last 10 incident postmortems and correlate them with architecture findings from previous reviews. Issues to find: (1) Incident #1 (database connection exhaustion, 2 hours downtime) — root cause was Review 08's connection pool math (6400 connections). The postmortem recommended 'increase max_connections' but didn't address the fundamental architecture issue. The fix MASKED the problem, which will recur when Review 15's plan adds more shards, (2) Incident #3 (payment double-charge affecting 47 customers, $23K refunded) — root cause was Review 14's exactly-once illusion combined with Review 07's 24-hour deduplication TTL. The postmortem recommended 'increase Redis TTL to 7 days' but didn't address the fundamental dual-write problem from Review 01, (3) Incident #5 (PII data breach, 1200 customer records exposed via Elasticsearch) — root cause was Review 03's unauthenticated Elasticsearch read model. The postmortem added authentication to ES but didn't address the SAME unauthenticated access pattern in the data lake (Review 19), (4) Incident #8 (45-minute outage during deployment) — root cause was Review 31's migration framework running a blocking ALTER TABLE. The postmortem recommended 'test migrations in staging' but there IS no staging environment (Review 16's CI/CD finding), (5) each postmortem was written independently without cross-referencing other incidents — the systemic patterns (dual-write appears in 3 incidents, auth gaps in 4, capacity miscalculation in 3) were never identified because each postmortem treated its incident as isolated. This review must identify the SYSTEMIC patterns that individual postmortems missed.

tasks.title: Arch Review 35: Kubernetes Cluster Architecture With Blast Radius Concerns
tasks.task: Review the Kubernetes cluster architecture. Issues to find: (1) all 12 microservices, the API gateway (Review 04), the BFF (Review 18), the GraphQL federation gateway (Review 28), Redis (used by Reviews 05, 29, 30, 32), Kafka (Reviews 01, 07, 11, 17), Elasticsearch (Reviews 03, 12), and Vault (Review 10) run on the SAME Kubernetes cluster — a cluster-level incident (node kernel panic, etcd corruption) takes down EVERYTHING simultaneously, (2) the cluster uses a single namespace 'production' with no network policies — any pod can communicate with any other pod, which is how Review 09's Istio mTLS gap (pods starting without sidecars) leads to Review 33's plaintext communication risk, (3) the Kubernetes nodes use shared persistent volumes (EBS gp2) — the IOPS limit is shared between the application pods and the database pods. During Review 31's 4-hour data migration, the database consumes all IOPS, causing application pods to experience I/O timeouts, (4) the resource requests and limits are set based on the steady-state usage from Review 15's capacity plan — but Review 05's thundering herd, Review 10's hourly credential storms, and Review 30's WebSocket reconnection herds all create burst patterns that exceed the limits, causing OOMKills that trigger Review 30's reconnection thundering herd (circular failure), (5) the cluster autoscaler adds nodes based on CPU utilization — but most of the platform's bottlenecks are I/O-bound (database connections from Review 08, Redis operations from Review 32, Kafka throughput from Review 17), not CPU-bound. Autoscaling on CPU doesn't address the actual bottleneck.

tasks.title: Arch Review 36: Third-Party Integration Resilience With Cascading Failure Paths
tasks.task: Review the third-party integration layer. The platform integrates with: Stripe (Review 14), Twilio for SMS (Review 11), SendGrid for email, LaunchDarkly (Review 27), Auth0 for SSO, Datadog for monitoring (separate from Prometheus in Review 23), and Snowflake (Review 17). Issues to find: (1) there's no circuit breaker on ANY third-party integration — when Stripe has a 30-second latency spike, it cascades through the BFF (Review 18's 10-second timeout creates a mismatch), the saga coordinator (Review 22's 5-minute saga timeout), and the background job scheduler (Review 29's jobs pile up), creating a platform-wide degradation from a single vendor issue, (2) the Auth0 SSO integration stores the OIDC discovery document (.well-known) result in the L1 cache from Review 05 (5-min TTL). If Auth0 rotates their signing key, there's a 5-minute window where JWTs signed with the new key fail validation — combined with Review 27's feature flag cache delay, a coordinated Auth0 key rotation + kill-switch toggle could both be delayed by 5 minutes, (3) the Datadog agent runs as a DaemonSet and consumes 500MB RAM per node × 20 nodes = 10GB of monitoring overhead — this RAM isn't in Review 15's capacity plan, (4) SendGrid's webhook for bounce notifications POSTs to the SAME unverified webhook handler pattern from Review 14 (no signature verification) — any POST to /webhooks/sendgrid is trusted, allowing an attacker to mark legitimate email addresses as bounced, preventing future email delivery, (5) the Snowflake integration from Review 17 holds a warehouse RUNNING during business hours ($4/hour × 10 hours × 22 days = $880/month) — but the analytics queries actually run for only 45 minutes per day, wasting 93% of the warehouse compute budget.

tasks.title: Arch Review 37: Testing Architecture With Coverage Illusion
tasks.task: Review the test architecture and coverage strategy. Issues to find: (1) the overall test coverage metric is 78% — but this is LINE coverage, not BRANCH coverage. The actual branch coverage is 41%, meaning 59% of conditional logic paths are untested. Specifically, NONE of the error-handling paths for the dual-write failures (Reviews 01, 14, 22) are tested because tests mock the Kafka producer to always succeed, (2) integration tests use a local single-node Redis (not a cluster) — but production uses Redis Cluster (Reviews 05, 29, 30, 32's shared Redis). The integration tests can't catch cluster-specific issues like slot migration, CROSSSLOT errors, or the multi-key operations that fail in cluster mode but work in standalone mode, (3) the test database is a single PostgreSQL instance — but production is sharded across 16 instances (Review 08). The scatter-gather queries, cross-shard joins, and connection pool issues from Review 08 don't exist in tests, (4) the performance tests (load testing with k6) run against a staging environment that has 1/10th the data and 1/4 the traffic of production — Review 05's thundering herd, Review 12's search query explosion, and Review 08's shard scatter-gather all perform linearly in staging but degrade non-linearly in production, (5) the test suite takes 45 minutes to run (Review 16's CI/CD pipeline overhead) and tests are NOT parallelized — engineers routinely skip the full test suite and run only 'their' service's tests, missing cross-service integration failures like those identified in Reviews 11, 17, 18, and 22.

tasks.title: Arch Review 38: Cost Architecture With Hidden Resource Waste Compound Effects
tasks.task: Review the cloud infrastructure cost architecture. Issues to find: (1) the 16 PostgreSQL shards from Review 08 are db.r6g.2xlarge instances ($4,608/month each) — but average CPU utilization is 8% because the bottleneck is connection count (Review 08), not compute. The instances are oversized for CPU and right-sizing to db.r6g.large ($1,152/month each) would save $55,296/year but reduce max_connections, exacerbating the connection pool issue, (2) the Elasticsearch cluster for search (Review 12) and CQRS read model (Review 03) uses 6 data nodes — but Review 12 identified the cluster is over-sharded (12 shards for 500K docs). Reducing to 3 nodes saves $18,000/year but reduces the query parallelism that masks the ML-scoring latency issue from Review 12, (3) the DR standby environment (Review 21) costs $12,000/month but Review 21 found the DR plan has 8 critical gaps — the company is paying $144,000/year for DR that won't actually work, (4) the 340 stale feature flags from Review 27 don't directly cost money, but the LaunchDarkly license is per-flag-evaluation: 340 flags × evaluations for EVERY request × 10K req/min = millions of extra evaluations/month, contributing to a $2,400/month LaunchDarkly bill (50% of which is for stale flags), (5) the Kafka cluster from Reviews 01, 07, 11, 17 retains messages for 7 days — but the analytics pipeline (Review 17) reads data within 1 hour. The remaining 6 days of retention is 'just in case' and consumes 450GB of SSD storage ($135/month). These individual findings total $80K+/year in waste, but they've never been reviewed TOGETHER because each system is budgeted separately by different teams.

tasks.title: Arch Review 39: Security Architecture Assessment Chaining All Vulnerability Findings
tasks.task: Review the security architecture by chaining vulnerability findings from ALL previous reviews into exploit scenarios. Exploit chains to identify: (1) CHAIN A (data exfiltration): Forge JWT (Review 02, hardcoded secret) → Gateway trusts it (Review 04, no signature verification) → Access file storage with predictable keys (Review 13, UUIDv1) → Enumerate files across tenants (Review 20, no tenant isolation at CDN level) → Download files via unsigned CloudFront URLs (Review 13). Total: 5-step chain, zero authentication needed, (2) CHAIN B (financial fraud): Create account → Trigger payment via API → Payment webhook handler has no signature verification (Review 14) → Send fake 'charge.succeeded' webhook → Payment recorded without actual charge → Receive goods with no payment, (3) CHAIN C (PII harvest): Access unauthenticated Elasticsearch (Review 03) → Query the CQRS read model containing financial data → Cross-reference with data lake (Review 19) which has PCI card numbers from Review 14's logging → Extract full customer profiles with card numbers, (4) CHAIN D (platform takeover): Exploit ArgoCD cluster-admin (Review 16) → Access ConfigMap with JWT secret (Review 02) and DB encryption key (Review 33) → Decrypt database at rest → Modify audit trail (Review 26, same DB) to cover tracks, (5) CHAIN E (persistent access): Create feature flag (Review 27, no code review for flags) → Flag enables a hidden admin endpoint with backdoor access → Feature flag evaluation uses unverified JWT tenant_id → Attacker can switch between tenants arbitrarily. Document how 25+ individual findings across 38 reviews combine into 5+ complete attack chains.

tasks.title: Arch Review 40: Operational Runbook Assessment With Knowledge Gaps
tasks.task: Review the operational runbooks for completeness against the platform's actual architecture. Issues to find: (1) the 'Database Connection Exhaustion' runbook says 'increase max_connections and restart' — but Review 08's sharded architecture means restarting triggers Review 10's Vault credential re-authentication across all 16 shards simultaneously, causing an authentication storm that the runbook doesn't mention, (2) the 'High Latency Alert' runbook has a single decision tree — but Review 23 found that latency can originate from 14 different components (gateway, mesh, cache, database, search, etc.) and the runbook only covers 3 (application code, database, and 'external dependency'), missing the Istio overhead (Review 09), cache thundering herd (Review 05), and cross-shard scatter-gather (Review 08), (3) the 'Deploy Rollback' runbook says 'revert the ArgoCD deployment' — but Review 44's GitOps approach (if it follows from Review 16) means revert is 'git revert and push', and Review 31's database migration can't be reverted by a git revert because the schema change is permanent, (4) the 'Payment Failure' runbook says 'check Stripe dashboard' — but Review 14 found that failures can occur AFTER Stripe succeeds (dual-write DB failure), and Review 22's saga coordinator can create payment states that don't exist in Stripe at all (compensation transactions), (5) there's NO runbook for: Vault unsealing (Review 10), Elasticsearch reindex (Reviews 03, 12), Kafka consumer group rebalancing (Reviews 01, 11, 17), feature flag incidents (Review 27), or cross-tenant data leakage (Review 20). The runbooks cover 20% of the actual failure modes identified across 39 reviews.

tasks.title: Arch Review 41: Developer Onboarding Architecture With Compounding Complexity
tasks.task: Review the developer onboarding experience for new engineers joining the platform team. Issues to find: (1) the onboarding documentation is 3 years old and references architecture that predates Reviews 08 (sharding), 09 (service mesh), 10 (Vault), and 28 (GraphQL federation) — a new engineer following the docs would set up a local environment that doesn't match production, (2) the local development environment uses docker-compose with a single Postgres, single Redis, and no Kafka — this means new engineers can't reproduce ANY of the distributed-system bugs identified in Reviews 05 (thundering herd), 08 (cross-shard queries), 10 (Vault bootstrap), or 30 (WebSocket reconnection storms), (3) the 'architecture overview' diagram shows 12 services but the actual service count is 30+ (including Review 04's gateway, Review 18's BFF, Review 28's GraphQL gateway, Review 29's Sidekiq workers, and various batch processors) — new engineers are surprised by services they've never seen in the docs, (4) there's no documentation of the cross-service data flows that create the compound issues: the Kafka topic from Review 01 that feeds Reviews 07, 11, and 17 isn't documented as a multi-consumer shared resource, (5) the estimated 'time to first productive PR' is 2 weeks — but Review 37's testing architecture means engineers spend their first week trying to understand why tests pass locally but fail in CI (because local tests use different infrastructure from Reviews 37's findings).

tasks.title: Arch Review 42: API Contract Evolution With Consumer-Driven Gaps
tasks.task: Review the API contract evolution process. Issues to find: (1) there are 0 consumer-driven contract tests — API changes are validated only by the PROVIDER's tests, but Review 24's versioning analysis found that consumers on v1, v2, and v3 have different expectations, and none are verified, (2) the OpenAPI spec is manually maintained and diverges from the actual API behavior — Review 28's auto-generated GraphQL schema exposes fields that the OpenAPI spec says don't exist, and Review 24's deprecated v1 endpoints are missing from the spec entirely, (3) the webhook payloads (Review 14 for payments, Review 36 for email bounces) have no published contract — consumers implemented webhook handlers based on EXAMPLES in documentation, and when the payload format changed (Review 24's v2 webhook issue), consumers broke silently, (4) the internal service-to-service APIs (between the 12 microservices behind Reviews 04, 09, and 28's boundaries) have no contracts AT ALL — changes are coordinated via Slack messages and 'deploy and see if it breaks', which is why Review 34's postmortems found 3 incidents caused by uncoordinated API changes, (5) the file storage service from Review 13 returns presigned URLs that EMBED the storage backend's domain — consumers have hardcoded URL pattern parsing, meaning a migration from S3 to GCS would break all consumers because the URL format changes, even though the CONTENT is the same.

tasks.title: Arch Review 43: Performance Profiling Revealing Hidden Compound Bottlenecks
tasks.task: Review performance profiling results from a production load test at 2x current traffic. Issues to find: (1) the checkout flow p99 latency is 4.2 seconds — breakdown: API gateway JWT parsing 5ms + Istio mesh overhead 42ms (Review 09, 7 hops) + BFF sequential service calls 800ms (Review 18, not parallelized) + payment processing 2.1s (Review 14, Stripe + dual-write) + saga coordination 900ms (Review 22, distributed locks) + notification dispatch 350ms (Review 11, Kafka publish). EACH component's latency is 'acceptable' in isolation but they SUM to an unacceptable total, (2) the Redis cluster hits 95% memory utilization at 2x traffic — cache (Review 05) uses 4GB, Sidekiq (Review 29) uses 2GB, WebSocket pub/sub (Review 30) uses 1GB, rate limiting (Review 32) uses 500MB, feature flags (Review 27) uses 200MB, session store (Review 18) uses 300MB = 8GB total on a 10GB cluster. At 2x traffic, cache alone doubles to 8GB, exceeding capacity, (3) the database connection count at 2x traffic: 12,800 shard connections (Review 08 doubled) + 2,560 analytics (Review 17 doubled) + 500 migration framework (Review 31) = 15,860, requiring pgBouncer that isn't deployed (Review 15's gap), (4) the Kafka consumer lag for notifications (Review 11) is 45 seconds at 2x traffic because the SMS rate limit (100 msg/sec) becomes the bottleneck — the lag cascades to the analytics pipeline (Review 17) which reads the same topics, (5) the test suite from Review 37 didn't catch ANY of these compound bottlenecks because it tests at 1/10 scale where none of the non-linear effects manifest.

tasks.title: Arch Review 44: Zero-Trust Migration With Service Identity Chaos
tasks.task: Review the proposed zero-trust architecture migration. Issues to find: (1) zero-trust requires service identity — but Review 09's service mesh provides identity via SPIFFE IDs ONLY when the Istio sidecar is running. Review 35's finding that pods can start WITHOUT sidecars means services can operate with NO identity, bypassing zero-trust entirely, (2) the proposed per-service authorization policies need to know WHICH services call WHICH other services — but this call graph is undocumented. Review 41's onboarding gaps mean even the platform team doesn't have a complete picture. The call graph from Review 43's performance profiling only covers the checkout flow (7 of 30+ services), (3) the zero-trust migration must be incremental (can't enforce policies cluster-wide at once) — but the 'permissive mode' (allow all traffic, log violations) generates 2M log entries/day because the EXISTING traffic patterns include the unauthenticated flows from Reviews 03 (Elasticsearch), 04 (JWT non-verification), and 13 (unsigned CloudFront URLs), all of which would be logged as violations, (4) the proposal uses mutual TLS for service-to-service auth — but Review 33 found that Kafka (Reviews 01, 07, 11, 17) and the background jobs (Review 29) communicate in plaintext. Adding mTLS to Kafka requires client certificate distribution to every service that produces/consumes events, which depends on Review 10's Vault (for cert issuance), creating another Vault dependency, (5) zero-trust for the API gateway (Review 04) means validating JWT signatures AT the gateway — but the gateway currently delegates this to backend services (most of which skip it, per Review 04). Enforcing at the gateway breaks the 3 services that intentionally accept unsigned tokens for public endpoints.

tasks.title: Arch Review 45: Data Consistency Model Across All Storage Systems
tasks.task: Review the end-to-end data consistency model. The platform uses 7 data stores, each with different consistency guarantees, and NO overarching consistency strategy. Map the consistency issues: (1) PostgreSQL shards (Review 08): strong consistency per-shard, but the 2-phase commit coordinator from Review 22 provides 'dubious' cross-shard consistency (coordinator crash = stuck transactions), (2) Redis cache (Reviews 05, 29, 30, 32): eventual consistency by design, but the 5-minute L1 cache TTL means 5 minutes of potential stale reads. Combined with the BFF's session state (Review 18), a user may see their cart from 5 minutes ago, (3) Elasticsearch (Reviews 03, 12): near-real-time search (1-second refresh interval) but stale for up to 10 minutes during bulk reindex (Review 12). The CQRS read model (Review 03) can show phantom records from rolled-back transactions, (4) Kafka (Reviews 01, 07, 11, 17): ordered per-partition but the analytics pipeline (Review 17) and notification service (Review 11) can consume events at different rates, meaning the analytics view may be ahead of or behind the notification service's view, (5) S3/data lake (Reviews 13, 19): strongly consistent for individual objects but the data lake's 6 source tables (Review 19) are loaded at different times — a cross-table query may join Monday's payment data with Tuesday's user data. The compound effect: a user who places an order may see DIFFERENT states depending on WHICH service they query, for up to 10 minutes after the order, with no mechanism for any service to tell the user 'this data may be stale.'

tasks.title: Arch Review 46: Regulatory Compliance Mapping Against Technical Architecture
tasks.task: Review regulatory compliance (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOC 2) mapped against the ACTUAL technical architecture. Issues to find: (1) PCI-DSS Requirement 3 (protect stored cardholder data): violated by Review 14's payment logging (full card numbers), Review 19's data lake (card numbers replicated), Review 26's audit trail (captures payment log content), and Review 17's analytics pipeline (card numbers in Snowflake staging tables). The violation exists in 4 SEPARATE systems, each owned by a different team, (2) GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure): cannot be fulfilled due to Review 06's immutable event store, Review 19's data lake (immutable S3 partitions), Review 26's audit trail (7-year retention vs deletion right), and Review 17's Snowflake tables (data replicated for analytics). Erasure requires coordinated deletion across 4+ storage systems with no shared identifier for 'all data belonging to user X', (3) HIPAA (security rule): violated by Review 02's hardcoded JWT secret (access control failure), Review 33's plaintext Kafka (transmission security), Review 03's unauthenticated Elasticsearch (minimum necessary standard), and Review 10's 90-minute Vault unsealing delay (availability), (4) SOC 2 (CC6.1 logical access): violated by Review 16's ArgoCD cluster-admin, Review 35's single-namespace-no-network-policies, Review 27's feature flag changes bypassing review, and Review 20's application-layer-only tenant isolation, (5) the compliance audit is scheduled in 3 months — the remediation required across 46 review findings exceeds the 3-month timeline, requiring a risk-based prioritization that accounts for finding INTERACTIONS (fixing encryption in Review 33 doesn't help if Review 03's Elasticsearch is still unauthenticated).

tasks.title: Arch Review 47: Deprecation Strategy for Legacy Components
tasks.task: Review the deprecation strategy for legacy components identified across previous reviews. Issues to find: (1) Review 24's API v1 can't be deprecated because the largest customer (40% revenue) won't migrate — but Review 20 found v1 lacks tenant isolation, and Review 39's Chain A exploit works primarily through v1 endpoints. The business risk of keeping v1 (security) vs removing v1 (revenue) is a C-level decision but the architecture team has never quantified the security risk financially, (2) Review 27's 290 stale feature flags need deprecation but the flags reference code paths that may still be live via Review 24's v1 API — removing a flag that's 'stale' for v2 but 'active' for v1 would break the underprecated v1, (3) Review 31's migration from numbered-to-timestamp naming convention left 250 numbered migrations in the codebase. The Flyway migration history table has these as 'applied' — deleting the migration files doesn't clear the history, and new engineers are confused by the gap between migration files and Flyway's state table, (4) the docker-compose local development setup from Review 41 references deprecated container images (MongoDB for a feature removed 2 years ago, Memcached replaced by Redis) — removing them would 'break' the local setup for engineers who cloned the repo before the migration, but keeping them wastes 2GB of Docker images per engineer, (5) the deprecation timeline for Istio's mirrored queues (Review 07's RabbitMQ finding) requires migrating to quorum queues — but the migration path requires draining all messages from mirrored queues, which would lose the in-flight events that Review 21's DR plan depends on.

tasks.title: Arch Review 48: Scalability Model With Non-Linear Degradation Points
tasks.task: Review the platform's scalability model at 5x, 10x, and 50x current traffic. For each scale point, identify which previous review findings become BREAKING: (1) At 5x: Review 08's connection pool (6,400 × 5 = 32,000, exceeding even pgBouncer limits), Review 05's cache thundering herd (5x more concurrent cache misses), Review 32's centralized rate limiting (50K INCR/sec on single Redis key), Review 30's WebSocket connections (50K concurrent, exceeding single-server file descriptor limits). The platform breaks at 5x due to connection count alone, (2) At 10x: Review 12's Elasticsearch scoring (1000 HTTP calls/query at 10x result volume), Review 17's analytics pipeline (12,800 DB connections for enrichment × 10 = 128,000), Review 06's event replay (28,470 events per aggregate, 120-second replay time blocking request threads), Review 29's Sidekiq queue depth (queue grows faster than workers can process, leading to unbounded memory growth in Redis from Review 43), (3) At 50x: Review 08's sharding requires 80+ shards (hash function change, full data migration from Review 31), Kafka partition count must increase proportionally (topic recreation, consumer group rebalancing from Review 17), the DNS resolution for service mesh (Review 09) at 50x pods creates control plane bottleneck, (4) the NON-LINEAR degradation: most findings degrade worse-than-linearly. Review 08's scatter-gather is O(shards) per query × queries — at 50x this is O(shards²) because more shards means more scatter-gather. Review 05's thundering herd probability grows with cache miss rate which grows with access pattern diversity which grows with user count, (5) NO review finding actually scales linearly — identify the 3 findings with the WORST non-linearity and propose architecture changes.

tasks.title: Arch Review 49: Architecture Decision Record Audit Against Actual Outcomes
tasks.task: Review all Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) against the ACTUAL outcomes observed across 48 previous reviews. Issues to find: (1) ADR-001 chose event sourcing (Review 06) for 'auditability' — but Review 26 found the event store doesn't provide SOC 2 compliant audit trails, Review 06 found PII can't be deleted from the immutable store (GDPR violation), and Review 19 found phantom events replicated to the data lake. CQRS added operational complexity without achieving its stated performance goal, (3) ADR-008 chose hash-based sharding (Review 08) for 'horizontal scalability' — but Review 08 found scatter-gather query explosion, Review 15 found capacity planning errors, Review 17 found connection count explosion, Review 31 found migration complexity, and Review 48 found non-linear degradation. The sharding decision created more problems than it solved, (4) ADR-012 chose Redis for 'caching, queuing, pub/sub, and sessions' — BUT Reviews 05, 18, 27, 29, 30, and 32 found that a SINGLE Redis cluster serving 6 different purposes creates resource contention and cascading failures, (5) ADR-015 chose microservices for team independence — but Reviews 11, 17, 22, and 42 found that inter-service dependencies create implicit coupling that microservices didn't solve, and Review 37 found that testing in isolation hides integration failures. Audit ALL ADRs against their actual outcomes documented across 48 reviews.
